title: Genie AI Assistant
excerpt: What Genie AI Assistant Does Genie is an AI-powered assistant built directly into GCXONE. It lets you query platform data, manage tasks, and get operational insights through a simple conversational interface - without navigating menus or switching sc
content:
What Genie AI Assistant Does Genie is an AI-powered assistant built directly into GCXONE. It lets you query platform data, manage tasks, and get operational insights through a simple conversational interface - without navigating menus or switching screens. It lives as a floating panel on your dashboard, always one click away.
Why It Matters Operations move fast. Digging through multiple screens to find an alarm summary or check a ticket status slows you down. Genie removes that friction. You ask, it answers - instantly, in context, without interrupting your workflow.
How It Works Click the Genie icon to open the floating assistant panel. Genie greets you and displays five quick-access topic tabs aligned with your most common workflows. Select a topic or type your query directly. Genie processes it and returns the relevant data, summaries, or action options right inside the panel.
figure
Key Capabilities Genie provides five built-in topic shortcuts to help you move fast:
Spawn: Initiate or trigger platform actions quickly
Insights: Get summaries and analysis of alarms and system data
Ticket Management: Open tickets, close tickets by number, check ticket status, or get a ticket categories summary
VPN Requests: Submit and manage VPN-related requests through the assistant
Device Setup and Troubleshooting: Get step-by-step guidance on device configuration and connectivity issues Beyond shortcuts, Genie lets you:
Retrieve system information on demand.
Access alarm and event insights without navigating the dashboard.
Manage tickets and VPN requests in one place.
Troubleshoot device setup and connectivity issues.
Make faster operational decisions with immediate data access.
Real-World Use Cases
An operator receives a high volume of alarms. They ask Genie to summarize alarm activity - distinguishing passed alarms from filtered alerts - without reviewing each entry manually.
A technician handles repeated connection failures at a site. They ask Genie to open a support ticket, check an existing ticket by number, or get a summary of open ticket categories - all without leaving the dashboard.
A new site requires remote access. A user submits a VPN request directly through Genie instead of going through a separate process or contacting support.
A supervisor needs a quick overview of camera health across sites. Genie surfaces data on healthy cameras, connection failures, and obstructed cameras - helping teams prioritize where attention is needed.
A field engineer deploys a new device. They ask Genie for step-by-step configuration guidance and resolve connectivity issues on the spot.
Additional Details You can ask Genie about alarm summaries, ticket status, VPN requests, camera health, and device troubleshooting. You can also type any platform-related question directly - no topic tab required. The topic tabs are shortcuts, not requirements. Type your question at any time and Genie retrieves the relevant information. If Genie does not have the answer, it will let you know. Contact support for any information outside the platform's operational data.

title: AI Analytics Overview
excerpt: AI Analytics is one of GCXONE's most powerful features, using intelligent algorithms to analyze video footage and distinguish real security threats from false alarms. Security operations often struggle with high volumes of false alarms generated by a
content:
AI Analytics is one of GCXONE's most powerful features, using intelligent algorithms to analyze video footage and distinguish real security threats from false alarms. Security operations often struggle with high volumes of false alarms generated by animals, lighting changes, shadows, and environmental factors — pulling operator attention away from genuine threats. GCXONE's AI Analytics solves this by reducing false alarms by up to 80%, ensuring critical alarms are processed within strict service level agreements.
Key Capabilities
AI-Powered False Alarm Filtering Traditional motion detection systems generate numerous false alarms from:
Animals and environmental factors such as wind and shadows
Lighting changes and camera movement or vibration
Reflections and glare GCXONE's AI Analytics uses advanced computer vision algorithms to analyze video frames and identify actual security threats versus false triggers — reducing false alarms by up to 80%.
Detection Capabilities Centralized Alarm Processing
System receives alarms from connected devices
AI algorithms analyze associated video footage
Alarms classified as real (human or vehicle detected) or false (no threat identified)
Processed alarms distributed to operators based on customer preferences
Unclear cases forwarded for operator verification
Strict Service Level Agreements (SLA) 60–90 Second Processing Window GCXONE is engineered to process alarms within a strict 60–90 second window, ensuring rapid response to genuine security threats. Automatic Escalation If the AI cannot process an alarm within the SLA timeframe, it automatically forwards it as a real alarm for human review — no genuine threat is ever missed. Priority Classification The system automatically classifies alarms based on:
Detection confidence levels
Threat type and alarm source
Historical patterns and context

title: Alarm Management System
excerpt:
What Alarm Management System Does Connect GCXONE directly to your Central Monitoring Station. The Alarm Management System supports DC-09 and Evalink Talos integrations. Alarms forward automatically the moment they trigger.
Why It Matters Monitoring c
content:
What Alarm Management System Does Connect GCXONE directly to your Central Monitoring Station. The Alarm Management System supports DC-09 and Evalink Talos integrations. Alarms forward automatically the moment they trigger.
Why It Matters Monitoring centres depend on fast, accurate alarm delivery. Manual forwarding introduces delays. Errors lead to missed incidents. The Alarm Management System eliminates that gap. GCXONE forwards alarms to the CMS the moment they trigger - automatically and reliably.
How It Works
DC09 CMS Integration What is SIA DC-09? SIA DC-09 is the industry-standard protocol for transmitting alarm signals over IP networks. All major CMS platforms support it. GCXONE extends the DC-09 signal with a secure event link. Opening the link shows pre-event footage, the live feed, and alarm metadata. Step 1 - Navigate to the Marketplace Go to Marketplace from the left navigation. Click the Alarm Management System tab to filter available integrations. _Figure 1: Marketplace Navigation - Filter to Alarm Management System to see all supported CMS receivers._ The Alarm Management System section displays all supported receivers. Each card shows the connection state. _Figure 2: Integration Status - Each card displays the receiver connection state: Configured or Not Configured._ Step 2 - Configure the CMS Receiver Click Explore on the CMS you want to configure. Each integration page describes the connection scope and required fields. _Figure 3: Integration Detail - Review the connection scope before clicking Configure._ The configuration dialog collects two pieces of information to establish the DC-09 connection:
DC09 Receiver IP Address - the destination endpoint where GCXONE will push alarm data. This is the IP address of the CMS server.
DC09 Receiver Port - the TCP port the CMS server is listening on for incoming DC-09 signals. _Figure 4: Connection Setup - Enter the IP address and port to route GCXONE alarms to your CMS._ The same dialog applies to all DC-09 receivers. For unlisted systems, use Other CMS System to connect any SIA DC-09 compatible receiver. _Figure 5: Custom CMS Setup - Connect any SIA DC-09 compatible receiver not listed in the Marketplace._ Once submitted, GCXONE establishes the TCP connection. The card updates to Configured. Alarms start forwarding immediately. After configuring the receiver, a new Alarm Management System tab appears. Navigate to Configuration to access the site mapping table. After configuring the receiver, a new Alarm Management System tab appears. Navigate to Configuration > [Your Service Provider] > The table lists all sites under that provider - Customer, Site, DC09 Account ID, assigned receiver, and connection status. _Figure 6: Site Mapping Table - Red indicators show sites not yet connected to a CMS receiver._ Sites successfully mapped show a green indicator and the name of the CMS they are connected to. _Figure 7: Mapping Progress - Green sites are active and forwarding alarms; red sites need configuration._ Click the edit icon on any site row to open the Edit IP Configuration dialog. Assign a DC09 Account ID to link the site to its CMS account. Click the edit icon on any site row to open the configuration dialog. This step maps each site to its CMS account. _Figure 8: Site Mapping - Assign a DC09 Account ID to link each site to its CMS account._ The dialog requires two fields:
DC09 Account ID - this must exactly match the site name as registered on the CMS. It is case-sensitive.
Encryption Key - an optional shared secret for encrypted DC-09 communication. Leave blank if encryption is not required. Click Update to save. GCXONE forwards alarms via DC-09 with a secure event link. Operators at the CMS open the link to review the full alarm context.
Evalink Talos Integration What is Evalink Talos? Evalink Talos is a cloud-native alarm management platform for monitoring centres. Unlike DC-09, it uses a REST API for alarm delivery. Configuring the Evalink Talos Integration In the Marketplace, click Explore on the Evalink Talos card. The detail page explains the connection scope and required credentials. _Figure 9: Evalink Talos Card - A green badge confirms the integration is active and forwarding alarms._ The Evalink Talos configuration requires two values, both obtained from your Talos account:
API Key - a Talos-generated key with full access permissions. This authenticates GCXONE with your Talos instance.
Company ID - the unique identifier for your organization within Talos. This ensures alarms route to the correct account. Find both values in your Talos account under API Access. Paste them into the form and click Configure.
Real-World Use Cases
DC-09 monitoring centres - route alarms to Amwin, Lisa, Immix, or any SIA DC-09 receiver directly from GCXONE.
Evalink Talos users - consolidate GCXONE alarm data into Talos for a single source of truth across all monitored sites.
Multi-site service providers - map hundreds of sites to their CMS accounts in one table.

title: Auto Stream
excerpt: Overview Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature that automatically opens live camera feeds when an intrusion alarm is triggered at a site. It is designed for installations where alarm panels — infrared motion detectors, door contacts, or other sensor-based
content:
Overview Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature that automatically opens live camera feeds when an intrusion alarm is triggered at a site. It is designed for installations where alarm panels — infrared motion detectors, door contacts, or other sensor-based devices — are connected to Talos but have no video capability of their own. When one of these panels raises a burglary (BA) or intrusion alarm, Auto Stream bridges the gap: it instructs GCXONE to immediately begin streaming the cameras associated with the triggered zone, giving operators instant visual context without any manual action. The feature is available in the Video Viewer application via the Auto Stream toggle. It works in combination with the zone mapping configured between Talos and GCXONE, and relies on the NXGEN Genesis–Talos Bridge browser extension to relay alarm messages between the two platforms.
How It Works When an intrusion alarm arrives in Talos, the platform sends a trigger message through the Genesis–Talos Bridge to GCXONE. This message includes the zone identifier that raised the alarm. GCXONE looks up which cameras are mapped to that zone and starts streaming them automatically for the operator working the alarm. The full logic is as follows:
Matching zone found: only the cameras mapped to that zone are streamed.
No matching zone, or no zone in the alarm message: the entire site streams — all cameras across all devices at that site.
Multiple alarms in one workflow: GCXONE aggregates the camera lists from all zones referenced across all alarms. Duplicate cameras are not repeated. If one zone has no mapping, the entire site is included in the stream.
Motion alarm present in the same workflow: motion alarms take precedence. Quad view (multi-monitor) is shown instead of Auto Stream. If Auto Stream is on a separate screen, it continues streaming concurrently.
Dynamic refresh: the stream updates automatically if new alarms are attached to the workflow while it is being worked. If a motion alarm arrives, the screen switches to quad view.
:::note Important Zones must be created in Talos before they are configured in GCXONE. If zone configuration is done in the reverse order, the sync to Talos will fail. Additionally, if no zone is configured in GCXONE, or no cameras match the zone in the alarm, the entire site will stream. :::
Prerequisites Before configuring Auto Stream, ensure the following are in place:
Genesis–Talos Bridge installed: this browser extension must be installed and active. It is the communication channel between Talos and GCXONE. All Talos customers will have this. Search for "Talos Genesis Bridge" in browser extensions if not already installed.
NXGEN Genesis integration enabled in Talos: The Genesis integration must be active under Company → Integrations in Talos, with the correct service provider ID and access tokens configured.
Auto Stream alarm codes configured: in Talos under Company → Integrations → NXGEN Genesis, specify which alarm codes should trigger Auto Stream (typically burglary alarms, code BA or BA 130).
Configuration Steps Step 1 — Enable the NXGEN Genesis Integration in Talos In Talos, navigate to Company in the left navigation, then open the Integrations tab. Locate the NXGEN Genesis integration and confirm it is enabled (shown with a green checkmark). This integration is what allows Talos to send Auto Stream triggers to GCXONE. _Figure 1: Talos Company → Integrations tab. The Virtual SIA DC-09 Receiver is also visible here as a separate integration._ _Figure 2: NXGEN Genesis integration confirmed as Enabled. This must be active for Auto Stream triggers to reach GCXONE._ Step 2 — Configure Auto Stream Alarm Codes in the Genesis Integration Within the NXGEN Genesis integration settings in Talos, scroll down to the alarm stream configuration section. Here you define which alarm codes should trigger Auto Stream. Burglary alarm code BA (also referred to as BA 130) is the typical code used for intrusion panel alarms. Any alarm arriving in Talos with one of these codes and associated with a configured site will trigger the Auto Stream flow in GCXONE. Step 3 — Create Zones in Talos For each site where Auto Stream will be used, navigate to the site in Talos and open the Zones & Users tab. Add the zones that correspond to the physical areas of the site — for example, Zone 1 for the front entrance, Zone 2 for the server room, and so on. Assign a Zone ID and Zone Name to each. The Zone ID is what GCXONE will use to match incoming alarm messages to specific cameras. _Figure 3: Talos site overview showing the site selected in the left panel. Navigate to the site before accessing Zones & Users._ _Figure 4: Talos site detail view. The Zones & Users tab is accessible from the top navigation within the site. Virtual SIA DC-09 Receivers are also visible here._ _Figure 5: Zones & Users configuration in Talos. Each zone has a Zone Type, Zone ID, and Zone Name. These Zone IDs must be created here before configuring sensors in GCXON

title: HealthCheck
excerpt:
What HealthCheck Does HealthCheck automatically monitors every connected camera in your fleet. It verifies each camera is reachable, streaming, and delivering a valid image. The feature is built around three goals:
Validate that all cameras are onlin
content:
What HealthCheck Does HealthCheck automatically monitors every connected camera in your fleet. It verifies each camera is reachable, streaming, and delivering a valid image. The feature is built around three goals:
Validate that all cameras are online and functioning correctly.
Deliver proactive alerts for failed or degraded feeds.
Give operators flexible control via scheduling, manual execution, and hierarchy-based configuration.
Why It Matters Large camera networks fail silently. Cameras go offline, get obstructed, or get tampered with. No one notices until an incident exposes the gap. HealthCheck eliminates that risk.
Camera connectivity loss or total offline status.
Blocked, obstructed, or vandalized lenses.
Low-light or night conditions making video unusable.
Overexposed or washed-out images from glare or direct light.
Camera movement or deliberate tampering.
Software-level failures - no license, no signal. Without automated monitoring, these issues go undetected. Coverage gaps grow until an incident exposes them. _Figure 1: HealthCheck - what it does, productivity impact, vulnerabilities closed and growth impact_
How It Works When HealthCheck runs, the platform executes the following steps:
Connects to the camera live stream.
Captures a snapshot from the feed.
Analyzes the image against configured checks.
Generates a health verdict - healthy, low light, obstructed, and more.
Records and displays results in the platform interface. HealthChecks run automatically on a schedule or on demand. Operators trigger manual checks from the interface at any time.
HealthCheck Modes Three monitoring modes are available. Each is designed for a different level of operational need: _Figure 5: HealthCheck Plans - feature comparison across Basic (Foundational), Plus (Proactive) and Advanced (Predictive)_
Key Capabilities
Continuous Camera Monitoring - Periodically verifies each camera is reachable and delivering a valid stream.
Automatic Failure Detection - Identifies cameras that are offline, unreachable, or delivering degraded feeds.
Image Condition Analysis - Detects low light, glare, obstructions, and conditions affecting video usability.
AI-Based Anomaly Detection - In Advanced mode, AI models analyze snapshots to detect tampering, tilt, and obstruction.
Flexible Scheduling - Run checks automatically at configurable intervals or trigger them on demand.
Hierarchical Configuration - Apply settings at any level of the platform hierarchy. From Service Provider down to individual sensors.
Centralized Dashboard - Color-coded indicators give instant visibility into healthy and failed cameras across all sites.
Visual Indicators and Status Display The HealthCheck dashboard is in the left sidebar (eye icon). Summary cards show checked cameras, healthy devices, connection failures, and detected issues. Cameras display in grid or list view. Each shows its health status and last check timestamp. Use filters to narrow results quickly:
Status filter - Healthy, Failed, or No Reference Image.
Camera Type filter - narrow results to a specific device or sensor type.
Include Inactive Sensors - show cameras that are not currently active.
Include Excluded Sensors - show cameras manually excluded from HealthCheck.
Generate Report - create a downloadable HealthCheck report for the selected customer. Compliance Reporting - A customer requires monthly proof that all cameras were operational. HealthCheck generates the report automatically and delivers it on schedule.

title: Nova99x
excerpt: What Nova99x Does Nova99X is GCXONE's intelligent false-alarm filtering module for surveillance operations. It processes every motion-triggered event in real time using computer-vision analysis - classifying each event as a true or false alarm before
content: What Nova99x Does Nova99X is GCXONE's intelligent false-alarm filtering module for surveillance operations. It processes every motion-triggered event in real time using computer-vision analysis - classifying each event as a true or false alarm before it ever reaches an operator. Nova99X suppresses up to 99% of false alarms, enabling security operations to scale without increasing headcount. Unlike ZenMode - which supports historical analysis and review - Nova99X operates inline on incoming alarms and controls what passes through the system in real time. Why It Matters Modern surveillance deployments generate enormous volumes of motion triggered alarms. The vast majority are noise from swaying trees, passing headlights, animals, weather changes, and environmental artifacts. Without filtering, operators spend most of their time reviewing junk alarms instead of real threats. Alarm fatigue causes genuine incidents to be missed or delayed. Scaling operations requires linear staffing increases that erode margins. Customer trust drops when false alert rates stay high. Nova99X solves this by removing noise before it reaches humans - so every alarm that lands in your operators' queue has already been validated by AI. How It Works Nova99X processes motion events the moment they are generated. It runs each event through a computer-vision pipeline that classifies it as a true or false alarm in real time - before any operator sees it. Only confirmed events are surfaced to your team. Everything else is suppressed automatically, with no manual triage and no delay. Configuration changes take effect immediately and apply to all incoming alarms at the selected entity level. Key Capabilities Nova99X runs three core algorithms to achieve this: Advanced Object Detector - achieves state-of-the-art performance in object detection benchmarks. Dynamic Motion Detection - pinpoints and analyzes movement within the relevant region of an event, significantly reducing false positives from environmental noise. Tracking and Re-Identification (ReID) - tailored on surveillance footage to provide temporal context, enabling Nova99X to filter out stationary objects such as parked vehicles that trigger false motion alarms. Nova99X operates at every level of the GCXONE entity hierarchy. Lower-level entities automatically inherit configuration from higher levels unless explicitly overridden. A single service-provider-level policy covers entire operations with zero additional configuration. **** Real-World Use Cases Service provider-wide noise control: Deploy Nova99X at the service provider level to enforce a baseline alarm quality standard across all customers. Prevent any single noisy deployment from overwhelming shared operations teams. Customer-level alarm policies: Apply filtering policies tailored to a specific customer's risk tolerance, environment, or contractual requirements - without impacting any other customer. Site-level environmental filtering: Address unique environmental factors at a specific location - weather interference, complex lighting, or high-traffic areas - with site-specific configuration. Device and sensor precision control: Isolate and fine-tune filtering for problematic cameras or sensors without touching the rest of the site configuration. Large-scale expansion: Stabilize alarm volume during rapid onboarding of new customers or sites, preventing early overload and ensuring quality from day one. **** Best Practices Optical (RGB) cameras: Verify that all cameras meet the minimum technical requirements before activating Nova99X. Detection accuracy depends directly on image quality at the sensor level. Thermal cameras: Configure the sensor as "Thermal" in GCXONE before activating Nova99X. This activates the correct detection model. Thermal cameras detect temperature gradients - humans typically appear brighter and are detected with high sensitivity. If excessive contrast enhancement (CLAHE, AGC) introduces false detections, enable useContrastEnhancement for the sensor. Position cameras to minimize tall heat-emitting objects - chimneys, vents, and heated pipes - in the field of view, as they can resemble a person's vertical profile. Note that cats and other animals share similar heat profiles to humans from certain angles and may occasionally be misclassified. Night detection: Cameras without IR rely on ambient or built-in illumination and are best suited for indoor environments. Outdoor use may require additional lighting. Cameras with IR night vision have a reduced effective field of view in low-light mode - person detection requires a minimum bounding box area of 600 px2 in this mode. High-speed vehicle scenarios: For cameras monitoring roads where vehicles pass quickly or make sharp turns at high speed, configure carBypassD

title: Audio Routing & Conference Mode
excerpt: Audio Routing & Conference Mode GCXONE supports advanced audio routing logic that allows multiple operators and field devices to collaborate in real-time. This is primarily powered by a Twilio Conference Bridge, ensuring high-quality, low-latency dup
content:
Audio Routing & Conference Mode GCXONE supports advanced audio routing logic that allows multiple operators and field devices to collaborate in real-time. This is primarily powered by a Twilio Conference Bridge, ensuring high-quality, low-latency duplex communication.
Audio Routing Modes Routing behavior is determined by the Active status of the Audio Devices configured at any given site.
Conference Mode (Full Collaboration) Condition: All configured audio devices at the site are toggled to Active. Behavior: All active devices and the operator are connected via a central conference bridge. Best For: Command centers or guard booths with multiple intercoms and speakers that need to hear the same broadcast simultaneously.
Dial-In Mode (Direct Control) Condition: Exactly one audio device is Active — all others are Inactive. Behavior: The operator is linked directly to the single active device. All other hardware is ignored. Best For: Single-point office intercoms or dedicated phone-line integrations.
Device Audio Mode (Isolated) Condition: All audio devices at the site are Inactive. Behavior: The system uses the camera's default internal microphone and speaker via G.711 or AAC over RTSP. Best For: Basic cameras with built-in audio support but no external speaker system.
Configuration Steps
Step 1: Register a Genesis Audio Device
Log in to GCXONE → Configuration App
Select your Site and navigate to the Devices tab
Click Add and set the type to Genesis Audio
Enter a name and click Discover
The system will generate unique SIP credentials — use these to configure your IP Speaker or SIP Horn
Step 2: Activate the Conference Toggle
In the Site tree view, select the newly added Genesis Audio device
Toggle the Active switch to ON
Repeat for all devices you want to include in the conference bridge
Step 3: Initiate an Audio Call
Open the Video Viewer
Drag a sensor or camera from the tree view into a tile
Locate the Mic Icon — default state is red
Click the icon to initiate the call — the icon will turn blue once the conference is active
Important Notes Permissions: Audio functionality is only visible to users with the REMOTE_TWO_WAY_AUDIO permission enabled in their role. Local Mode: Unlike video recording, audio calls do not require the Local Mode service to be active — they are handled directly via the cloud SIP gateway. Bandwidth: High-fidelity audio adds approximately 64 kbps per participant. Ensure your network has sufficient overhead for multi-party conferences.

excerpt:

Key
content:
Local Mode Overview Local Mode allows the operator workstation to establish a direct, peer-to-peer (P2P) connection to a Network Video Recorder (NVR) or device, typically bypassing the central cloud streaming gateway for high-bandwidth functions.
Key Functionalities When fully supported by a device type, Local Mode enables the following features directly on the operator's workstation:
P2P Streaming — Fast, direct video streaming with minimal latency
Live Video — Real-time video monitoring bypassing cloud proxies
Playback — Review of archived footage directly from local device storage
Local SDK Audio — High-performance two-way audio and announcements via device SDK
Encrypted Streams — Handling of encrypted communication streams from the device
Local PTZ & I/O — Direct control of pan-tilt-zoom and relay operations
Applicability Local Mode is highly recommended for sites with multiple operator workstations and high camera counts to maintain local bandwidth efficiency.
Not Supported Device Types Local Mode is not supported or required for:
Hanwha-Techwin NVRs: All features managed via cloud integration
Genesis Audio (SIP/Twilio): Cloud-native by design
InnoVi AI Cloud: Purely cloud-based SaaS solution
Senstar / Viasys: Integration is exclusively cloud-based
Prerequisites
Admin Privileges: Required for installation of the Local Mode service
Network Routing: Workstations must have network access to the device IPs
Genesis Installer: The Local Mode service must be installed on every operator workstation

excerpt: What TimeSync Does TimeSync automatically synchronizes the time and timezone of every connected device across all your sites. It pushes the correct settings continuously - keeping every camera, alarm, and log locked to one verified timeline without a
content:
What TimeSync Does TimeSync automatically synchronizes the time and timezone of every connected device across all your sites. It pushes the correct settings continuously - keeping every camera, alarm, and log locked to one verified timeline without any manual intervention. Why It Matters Mismatched device timestamps create confusing timelines, missed details, and unreliable evidence. In a compliance audit or security investigation, a few seconds of drift can cost you hours - or far more. TimeSync eliminates that risk entirely. It delivers 100% device synchronization, cuts investigation time by 80%, and enables teams to monitor 200+ more sites without additional staff. For business leaders, accurate and defensible records translate to €10k-€100k in annual savings through avoided legal risk.
How It Works TimeSync connects to all devices managed within GCX-ONE and automatically pushes the correct time and timezone settings. The sync runs continuously, so devices stay accurate over time with no manual effort required. GCX-ONE uses the following NTP server for time synchronization: timel.nxgen.cloud Key Capabilities
100% Device Synchronization: Eliminates confusion from mismatched timestamps across all sites.
Automatic Timezone Management: Applies the correct timezone per site and per device.
Trusted Evidence: Every frame is accurately timestamped and legally defensible.
Seamless Sync at Scale: Works across thousands of devices simultaneously.
Zero Drift: No IT intervention required after setup.
Key Capabilities
100% Device Synchronization: Eliminates confusion from mismatched timestamps across all sites
Automatic Timezone Management: Applies the correct timezone per sit and per device
Trusted Evidence: Every frame is accurately timestamped and legally defensible
Seamless Sync at Scale: Works across thousands of devices simultaneously
Zero Drift: No IT intervention required after setup ****
Real-World Use Cases
A security team investigates an incident across multiple camera feeds from different sites. Because TimeSync keeps all devices aligned, they reconstruct the full timeline in minutes instead of hours.
An organization undergoes a compliance audit. TimeSync ensures all recordings carry accurate, verifiable timestamps - creating tamper-proof records that hold up without dispute.
A device restarts after a power outage and its clock resets. TimeSync automatically re-syncs it when it comes back online, preventing any gaps in the recording timeline.
A manager oversees operations across sites in different timezones. TimeSync ensures each device shows the correct local time, preventing cross-site confusion during investigations.
Additional Details Automatic time synchronization is currently supported for HikVision and Dahua devices only. :::note Limited Device Support For all other device types, the Timezone field is still available for manual configuration. However, automatic time synchronization via TimeSync is not supported outside of HikVision and Dahua. :::

title: AutoStream - Zone-Based Camera Streaming
excerpt:
AutoStream
BY NXGEN
Auto Stream Zone-Based Camera Streaming for Intrusion Alarms Feature Guide
2026 GCXONE Security Management Platform | NXGEN Technology AG © 2026 NXGEN Technology AG. All rights reserved.
*Overview* Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature
content:
AutoStream
BY NXGEN
Auto Stream Zone-Based Camera Streaming for Intrusion Alarms Feature Guide
2026 GCXONE Security Management Platform | NXGEN Technology AG © 2026 NXGEN Technology AG. All rights reserved.
*Overview* Auto Stream is a GCXONE feature that automatically opens live camera feeds when an intrusion alarm is triggered at a site. It is designed for installations where alarm panels — infrared motion detectors, door contacts, or other sensor-based devices — are connected to Talos but have no video capability of their own. When one of these panels raises a burglary (BA) or intrusion alarm, Auto Stream bridges the gap: it instructs GCXONE to immediately begin streaming the cameras associated with the triggered zone, giving operators instant visual context without any manual action. The feature is available in the Video Viewer application via the Auto Stream toggle. It works in combination with the zone mapping configured between Talos and GCXONE, and relies on the NXGEN Genesis–Talos Bridge browser extension to relay alarm messages between the two platforms.
*How It Works* When an intrusion alarm arrives in Talos, the platform sends a trigger message through the Genesis–Talos Bridge to GCXONE. This message includes the zone identifier that raised the alarm. GCXONE looks up which cameras are mapped to that zone and starts streaming them automatically for the operator working the alarm. The full logic is as follows: __• Matching zone found:__ only the cameras mapped to that zone are streamed. __• No matching zone, or no zone in the alarm message:__ the entire site streams — all cameras across all devices at that site. __• Multiple alarms in one workflow:__ GCXONE aggregates the camera lists from all zones referenced across all alarms. Duplicate cameras are not repeated. If one zone has no mapping, the entire site is included in the stream. __• Motion alarm present in the same workflow:__ motion alarms take precedence. Quad view (multi-monitor) is shown instead of Auto Stream. If Auto Stream is on a separate screen, it continues streaming concurrently. __• Dynamic refresh:__ the stream updates automatically if new alarms are attached to the workflow while it is being worked. If a motion alarm arrives, the screen switches to quad view.
*Important* Zones must be created in Talos before they are configured in GCXONE. If zone configuration is done in the reverse order, the sync to Talos will fail. Additionally, if no zone is configured in GCXONE, or no cameras match the zone in the alarm, the entire site will stream.
*Prerequisites* Before configuring Auto Stream, ensure the following are in place: __• Genesis–Talos Bridge installed:__ this browser extension must be installed and active. It is the communication channel between Talos and GCXONE. All Talos customers will have this. Search for "Talos Genesis Bridge" in browser extensions if not already installed. __• NXGEN Genesis integration enabled in Talos:__ the Genesis integration must be active under Company → Integrations in Talos, with the correct service provider ID and access tokens configured. __• Auto Stream alarm codes configured:__ in Talos under Company → Integrations → NXGEN Genesis, specify which alarm codes should trigger Auto Stream (typically burglary alarms, code BA or BA 130).
*Configuration Steps*
*Step 1 — Enable the NXGEN Genesis Integration in Talos* In Talos, navigate to Company in the left navigation, then open the Integrations tab. Locate the NXGEN Genesis integration and confirm it is enabled (shown with a green checkmark). This integration is what allows Talos to send Auto Stream triggers to GCXONE.
AutoStream __Figure 1: Talos Company → Integrations tab. The Virtual SIA DC-09 Receiver is also visible here as a separate integration.__
AutoStream __Figure 2: NXGEN Genesis integration confirmed as Enabled. This must be active for Auto Stream triggers to reach GCXONE.__
*Step 2 — Configure Auto Stream Alarm Codes in the Genesis Integration* Within the NXGEN Genesis integration settings in Talos, scroll down to the alarm stream configuration section. Here you define which alarm codes should trigger Auto Stream. Burglary alarm code BA (also referred to as BA 130) is the typical code used for intrusion panel alarms. Any alarm arriving in Talos with one of these codes and associated with a configured site will trigger the Auto Stream flow in GCXONE.
*Step 3 — Create Zones in Talos* For each site where Auto Stream will be used, navigate to the site in Talos and open the Zones & Users tab. Add the zones that correspond to the physical areas of the site — for example, Zone 1 for the front entrance, Zone 2 for the server room, and so on. Assign a Zone ID and Zone Name to each. The Zone ID is what GCXONE will use to match incoming alarm messages to specific cameras.

content:
Event Clips Overview Event Clips is a critical feature in GCXONE that automatically captures and retrieves short video segments around alarm events, providing operators with immediate context to verify and respond to security incidents. This feature ensures that operators have the exact video context needed without manually searching through hours of recorded footage.
Key Capabilities
Automatic Clip Capture
Pre-Alarm Recording: Event Clips automatically captures video footage before an alarm triggers — typically 5 seconds before. This provides crucial context showing what happened leading up to the event.
Post-Alarm Recording: The system continues recording after the alarm trigger — typically 5 seconds after — capturing the immediate aftermath and any ongoing activity.
Configurable Buffer Times: The pre- and post-alarm buffer periods are configurable and can be adjusted based on specific requirements.
Supported Devices Event Clips are automatically captured and retrieved for:
ADPRO: Full support for event clip recording and retrieval
Dahua: Automatic event clip capture and access
Hikvision: Integrated event clip functionality
Milestone: Event clip support through VMS integration Device requirements:
Device must support event-triggered recording
Recording must be enabled on the device
Device must maintain a recording buffer
Network connectivity required for clip retrieval
Configurable Alarm Codes Event Clips can be configured to capture clips for specific alarm codes:
All Alarm Codes: Capture clips for all alarm types
Specific Alarm Codes: Configure which alarm codes trigger clip capture
Motion Detection: Capture clips for motion events
Intrusion Detection: Capture clips for intrusion alarms
Tamper Detection: Capture clips for tamper events
How It Works
Device-Side Recording The device continuously records video to storage and maintains a rolling buffer for pre-alarm footage. When an alarm triggers, the device saves the pre-alarm buffer, continues recording for the post-alarm period, marks the clip with alarm event information, and makes it available for retrieval.
GCXONE Retrieval When an alarm is received, GCXONE automatically requests the event clip from the device, retrieves it via network, processes it for immediate playback, and makes it available in the alarm interface.
Clip Duration Standard configuration is typically 10 seconds total — 5 seconds before the alarm trigger and 5 seconds after. Custom configurations can be adjusted based on alarm type and severity, response time requirements, storage considerations, and network bandwidth.
Integration with Alarm Workflow
Device sends alarm to GCXONE
GCXONE automatically requests the associated clip
If AI is enabled, the clip is analyzed for human or vehicle detection
Alarm and clip are presented to the operator together
Operator views clip to verify the alarm
Operator makes decision and initiates appropriate response actions
Use Cases
Alarm Verification
Quickly verify if an alarm is a genuine threat
Immediately identify false alarms
Understand what happened before and after the alarm
Incident Investigation
First view of the incident scene
Understand the sequence of events
Collect and document video evidence
Integration with AI Analytics
AI analyzes clips to classify alarms
Bounding boxes show detected objects on clips
Confidence scoring provides AI confidence levels
False alarms filtered automatically before reaching operators
Configuration Options
Buffer Configuration
Pre-Alarm Duration: Default 5 seconds before alarm
Post-Alarm Duration: Default 5 seconds after alarm
Total Clip Duration: Typically 10 seconds combined
Alarm Code Selection
All Alarms: Capture clips for all alarm types
Specific Codes: Select specific alarm codes for clip capture
Priority-Based: Capture clips only for priority alarms
Storage and Retention
Clips stored temporarily for operator access
Retention period typically 30–90 days
Automatic cleanup of old clips based on configured policies
Limitations and Considerations
Device Support
Only supported devices can provide event clips
Device must have recording enabled and maintain a recording buffer
Requires network connectivity for retrieval
Storage Considerations
Devices need sufficient storage for buffer
High alarm rates generate many clips
Balance retention period vs. storage capacity
Network Requirements
Clips require bandwidth for retrieval
Network must be reliable for clip access
Multiple clips may be retrieved simultaneously
Best Practices
Ensure at least 5 seconds of pre-alarm buffer for adequate context
Include at least 5 seconds of post-alarm buffer
Focus clip capture on critical alarm types and avoid capturing every minor event
Regularly monitor device storage capacity and configure automatic cleanup
Review and adjust alarm code selection periodically

title: Live View Overview
excerpt:
What Live View Does Live View gives you real-time video monitoring across all integrated cameras in GCXONE. Watch live feeds from any connected site. Manage multiple streams simultaneously and respond to events as they happen.
Why It Matters Delayed
content:
What Live View Does Live View gives you real-time video monitoring across all integrated cameras in GCXONE. Watch live feeds from any connected site. Manage multiple streams simultaneously and respond to events as they happen.
Why It Matters Delayed visibility costs operators critical response time. Live View eliminates that gap by delivering immediate, uninterrupted streams to the Video Viewer. You see what is happening and act before it escalates.
How It Works Open the Device Explorer from the left panel to browse your connected camera tree. Cameras are organized by customer, site, and device. Find and launch any feed in seconds. Drag a sensor onto the grid or double-click it in the device tree. Streaming starts immediately. Stream controls - refresh, close, freeze, and resume - are available directly on each tile. Figure 1: Device Explorer and Live Feed - Shows the Device Explorer tree with grouped camera devices alongside an active fisheye stream and the full toolbar, giving operators fast access to any camera across all integrated sites.
Key Capabilities
Flexible Grid Layouts - Switch between 1x1, 2x2, 3x3, and 4x4 layouts to match your monitoring load. Change layouts at any time without interrupting active streams.
Stream Controls - Refresh, freeze, close, or resume any feed directly from the tile. Jump Back, Playback, and Share controls are available in the expanded single-camera view.
PTZ Controls - Pan, tilt, zoom, and reposition compatible cameras using on-screen controls. Call saved presets to return to predefined positions instantly. Milestone devices support Rectangle PTZ for optical zoom and Point to Center for recentering.
Event Review (4-Panel View) - The 4-panel view displays Live, Pre, Post, and Preview feeds side by side. You get full event context in a single screen.
Sensor Labels - Overlay sensor names on the video stream for instant identification.
Sensor Isolation and Privacy Masks - Isolate any sensor for a defined duration to maintain focus during an active incident. Apply privacy masks to redact sensitive areas of the frame permanently. Figure 2: Multi-Camera Grid View - Shows six active camera feeds displayed simultaneously with labeled tiles, giving operators a complete overview of all monitored zones at once. Figure 3: Video Viewer - Single Camera Focus - Shows the Video Viewer with a single expanded feed and the bottom toolbar for Jump Back, Playback, Share, and Freeze/Play controls, enabling focused review with full playback access. Figure 4: Event Review - 4-Panel View - Shows Live, Pre, Post, and Preview panes displayed together during an alarm session, enabling side-by-side comparison of real-time and recorded footage.
Real-World Use Cases
Alarm Verification - An alarm triggers at a remote site. The operator opens Live View and confirms whether a person is present within seconds. AI bounding boxes from Event Metadata provide additional context directly in the stream.
PTZ Tracking - Movement is detected at a perimeter camera. The operator uses PTZ controls to track the subject across the scene. After the incident, they call a preset to restore the default camera position.
Multi-Site Monitoring - A supervisor needs to check activity across multiple sites. Using a 3x3 grid, they load feeds from different locations into a single view. All sites are monitored in parallel.
Talos CMS Integration - An operator assigns an alarm in Talos CMS. Live View automatically opens the relevant camera in the Video Viewer. The operator has full visual context before taking any action. Figure 5: Fullscreen Camera Feed - Shows an individual camera in Fullscreen mode with Live and quality indicators, providing an unobstructed view for high-priority incident monitoring.
Best Practices
Organize camera groups in the Device Explorer by zone or priority. Operators find feeds faster under pressure when the tree reflects your site layout.
Call PTZ presets after every repositioning task. Cameras left off-position reduce coverage and create blind spots between incidents.
Apply privacy masks during initial configuration - not reactively. Pre-masking sensitive areas ensures compliance before any live event captures restricted zones.
Train operators on the 4-panel event view. Reviewing Live, Pre, Post, and Preview together reduces misidentification and speeds up alarm decisions.
Additional Details Live View support varies by manufacturer and deployment mode. Full support in both Cloud and Local modes: Adpro, Axis, Axxon, Dahua, Hikvision, Milestone. Cloud-only support: Hanwha, NetVue, Viasys. No live support: Reconeyez PIR Cams and Ajax PIR cameras. These devices are designed for event-driven snapshots and do not support continuous streaming. Streaming protocols in use:
RTSP: Axis, Axxon, Hanwha, Hikvision
MJPEG: Viasys
TCP JPEG / Raw: Milestone The operator account must have Live View or Operator permissions enabled on the source device. Required network ports - RTSP (554) and HTTP (80/443) - must be open an

title: Playback Overview
excerpt: The Playback feature in GCXONE enables users to review recorded video footage from any integrated camera or device. Whether investigating a security incident, reviewing alarm events, or analyzing historical footage, Playback provides the tools needed
content:
The Playback feature in GCXONE enables users to review recorded video footage from any integrated camera or device. Whether investigating a security incident, reviewing alarm events, or analyzing historical footage, Playback provides the tools needed to find the right footage quickly and efficiently — with synchronized multi-camera playback, event navigation, and advanced search capabilities.
Key Capabilities
Unified Timeline (Smart Timeline) Playback Controls Multi-Camera Playback
Synchronized Playback – View multiple cameras at the exact same point in time
Grid Views – Monitor multiple camera feeds simultaneously
Independent Controls – Control each camera's playback independently if needed
Comparison Mode – Compare views from different cameras side-by-side Search and Navigation
Time Search – Jump to a specific date and time instantly
Event Search – Search for specific event types such as motion and alarms
Calendar Navigation – Click on calendar dates to view recordings
Bookmarks – Save specific timestamps for quick access later Video Export
Export selected video segments in various formats
Choose export quality and resolution
Batch export multiple clips simultaneously
Download clips or save directly to cloud storage
How It Works
Video is recorded on edge devices such as cameras and NVRs
GCXONE indexes recording metadata and event information
A unified timeline is generated from multiple device recordings
When a user requests playback, GCXONE retrieves the video from device storage
Video is streamed directly to the user interface
Recording Sources Use Cases Incident Investigation When a security incident occurs, operators can review footage from the exact time and location of the event, analyze the sequence of events across multiple cameras, and export timestamped video clips as evidence. Event Review Operators can review alarm events to determine their cause, analyze motion detection triggers, and investigate false alarms to verify system performance and fine-tune detection settings. Forensic Analysis For legal or compliance purposes, investigators can perform detailed frame-by-frame analysis, reconstruct events using multi-camera footage, and export clips with full timestamps for official use. System Monitoring Administrators can use Playback to review recording quality and coverage across all devices, identify recording gaps or issues, and monitor storage usage and retention policies.
Recording Requirements For Playback to work properly, the following conditions must be met:
Recording must be enabled on devices
Sufficient storage space must be available on the device
Devices must be synchronized with an NTP server
A stable network connection between the device and GCXONE is required
A continuous or event-based recording schedule must be configured
Best Practices
Timeline Navigation
Use event markers to quickly find relevant footage
Use the calendar for navigating to specific dates
Bookmark important timestamps for quick access
Multi-Camera Review
Use synchronized playback for accurate event correlation
Compare views from different angles to reconstruct events
Focus on cameras most relevant to the specific event
Video Export
Export only the necessary segments to save storage
Include buffer time before and after the event
Organize exports with clear, descriptive file names
Performance Optimization
Limit the number of concurrent playback streams
Close unused playback windows when not in use
Use Local Mode for on-site workstations to reduce bandwidth usage
Limitations and Considerations
Network Bandwidth
Playback requires sufficient network bandwidth
Multiple concurrent streams increase bandwidth consumption
Consider Local Mode for high-bandwidth scenarios
Storage Availability
Playback is limited to available recorded footage
Retention policies affect how far back recordings are accessible
Recording gaps will limit playback availability in those periods
Device Compatibility
Playback features may vary by device type
Older devices may have limited playback capabilities
VMS integrations may have specific compatibility requirements

title: PTZ Control Configuration
excerpt: Video & Streaming - PTZ Control Configuration This guide covers the configuration of PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) control for cameras in GCXONE, including device-side setup, platform configuration, preset management, and automation. Estimated time: 20–30 minu
content:
Video & Streaming - PTZ Control Configuration This guide covers the configuration of PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) control for cameras in GCXONE, including device-side setup, platform configuration, preset management, and automation. Estimated time: 20–30 minutes per PTZ camera
Prerequisites Before configuring PTZ control, ensure:
PTZ camera is successfully added to GCXONE
Camera hardware supports PTZ functionality
Device is online and accessible
Video streaming is working correctly
Appropriate user permissions are available
Configuration Workflow
Device-Level PTZ Configuration
GCXONE PTZ Settings
Preset Configuration
Preset Tours
PTZ Automation
Verification
Part 1: Device-Level PTZ Configuration
Step 1: Enable PTZ on Camera Device Navigation: Device Web Interface → PTZ Settings / Camera Control
Access the camera's web interface
Navigate to PTZ Settings or Camera Control
Enable PTZ Function:
Enable PTZ and select appropriate protocol such as ONVIF or manufacturer-specific
Verify PTZ Address — typically 1 or auto-detect
Configure Movement Limits if applicable:
Set minimum and maximum pan and tilt angles
Configure maximum movement speeds
Configure PTZ Parameters:
Idle Time: Time before camera returns to home position
Home Position: Define default position
Click Save or Apply Note: Exact navigation varies by manufacturer. Refer to device documentation.
Step 2: Configure PTZ Protocol Settings Navigation: Device Web Interface → Network → PTZ / Protocol Settings
Verify PTZ Protocol — enable ONVIF PTZ for compatibility or native manufacturer protocol if available
Verify PTZ control port — typically the same as the device HTTP port
Ensure the integration user has PTZ control permissions
Test PTZ from device interface — verify pan, tilt, zoom, and preset save/recall
Click Save
Part 2: GCXONE Platform PTZ Configuration
Step 3: Enable PTZ Control in GCXONE Navigation: GCXONE → Devices → [Camera Name] → Camera Configuration → PTZ Settings
Navigate to Devices and select your PTZ camera
Click Camera Configuration and navigate to PTZ Settings tab
Enable PTZ Control and select protocol — Auto-detect or specific
Click Test PTZ Connection to verify communication
Configure PTZ Options:
Default Speed: Set default movement speed from 1–8
Invert Controls: Enable if needed for camera orientation
Relative Movement: Enable for incremental movement
Configure Advanced Settings:
Control Timeout: Time before control is released if multiple users
Enable Preset Support and Tour Support if applicable
Click Save Configuration
Step 4: Configure PTZ User Permissions Navigation: GCXONE → Settings → User Management → [User] → Permissions
Select user or role to configure
Enable PTZ Control permissions:
View PTZ Controls: to see PTZ interface
Control PTZ: to operate camera
Manage Presets: to create and edit presets
Control Tours: if using tours
Configure PTZ Restrictions if needed:
Restrict to specific cameras
Limit PTZ control to specific times
Click Save Permissions
Part 3: Preset Configuration
Step 5: Create Preset Positions Navigation: GCXONE → Live View → [PTZ Camera] → PTZ Controls → Presets
Navigate to Live View and select the PTZ camera
Open PTZ Controls panel
Use PTZ controls to position camera — pan, tilt, zoom, and focus as needed
Once positioned correctly:
Click Save Preset or the + button
Enter a descriptive preset name such as "Front Entrance" or "Parking Lot North"
Enter a preset number from 1–255
Click Save or OK
Verify preset appears in the list and test recalling it Best practices for presets:
Use descriptive names indicating location or purpose
Number critical presets sequentially from 1–10 for quick access
Test each preset after creation
Update presets if camera position changes
Step 6: Organize and Manage Presets Navigation: GCXONE → Devices → [Camera] → PTZ Settings → Preset Management
Edit Preset: Click on preset name to update or reposition
Reorder Presets: Drag and drop to reorder or renumber for logical organization
Delete Preset: Select preset, click Delete, and confirm
Export/Import Presets: Export for backup or import to duplicate on other cameras
Part 4: Preset Tours Configuration
Step 7: Create Preset Tours Navigation: GCXONE → Devices → [Camera] → PTZ Settings → Tours
Click Create Tour or Add Tour
Configure Tour Settings:
Tour Name: such as "Perimeter Scan" or "Entrance Check"
Enable Repeat for continuous tours
Add Presets to Tour:
Select presets to include
Set Dwell Time — how long to stay at each preset
Set Movement Speed between presets
Reorder presets to define tour sequence
Configure Tour Options:
Start Position, End Behavior, and whether the tour is interruptible
Click Save Tour
Step 8: Configure Tour Automation Navigation: GCXONE → Settings → Automation → PTZ Tours
Create Tour Schedule:
Select tour and set time of day and days of week
Configure Event-Triggered Tours:
Set trigger such as alarm type or time period
Set Tour Priorities — define which tours can interrupt others
Click Save Automation
Part 5: PTZ Automation

title: PTZ Control Overview
excerpt: Video & Streaming - PTZ Control Overview PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) Control enables remote operation of motorized cameras, allowing operators to adjust camera position, angle, and zoom level in real-time from the GCXONE interface. This feature is essential
content:
Video & Streaming - PTZ Control Overview PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom) Control enables remote operation of motorized cameras, allowing operators to adjust camera position, angle, and zoom level in real-time from the GCXONE interface. This feature is essential for active surveillance, investigation, and dynamic monitoring scenarios.
Key Capabilities
Basic PTZ Operations
Pan: Horizontal rotation left and right — typically ±360° continuous or limited range
Tilt: Vertical rotation up and down — typically ±90° or more depending on camera
Zoom: Optical and/or digital zoom for closer views
Focus: Automatic or manual focus control on supported cameras
Iris: Aperture control for light management on supported cameras
Advanced Features
Preset Positions: Save and recall up to 255 preset camera positions for quick access to critical viewing angles
Preset Tours: Automated sequences visiting multiple preset positions on a schedule
Pattern Recording: Record and replay movement patterns for consistent monitoring
Speed Control: Adjustable movement speed from 1–8 depending on device
Relative Movement: Move camera by specific increments for fine adjustments
Absolute Positioning: Move camera to exact coordinates on supported devices
Rectangle PTZ (Milestone-specific): Draw a rectangle on the screen to optically zoom into that specific area
Point to Center (Milestone-specific): Click a point on the screen to center the camera on that exact spot
Control Methods
On-Screen Controls: Click-and-drag or directional buttons in GCXONE interface
Keyboard Controls: Arrow keys for pan and tilt, +/- for zoom
Mobile App: Touch-based PTZ controls on mobile devices
Preset Selection: Quick access to saved preset positions
Supported Devices PTZ control is supported on PTZ cameras, PTZ domes, speed domes, and cameras with built-in PTZ functionality. Device requirements:
Camera must have PTZ hardware capabilities
Device must support PTZ protocol such as ONVIF or manufacturer SDK
PTZ functionality must be enabled in device settings
Appropriate user permissions required
Supported Protocols
ONVIF PTZ: Standard protocol for PTZ control
Manufacturer SDKs: Native protocols for Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, and others
Pelco-D/P: Legacy protocol support
HTTP/HTTPS: Web-based PTZ control
How It Works
Operator initiates PTZ command via GCXONE interface
GCXONE translates command to device protocol
Command sent to camera via network
Camera motor executes movement
Camera position and status updated in interface
Use Cases
Active Surveillance
Monitor moving subjects across large areas
Follow suspicious activity in real-time
Respond to alarms by positioning camera on event location
Investigation
Zoom in on specific details or areas
Capture multiple angles of incident scene
Document evidence with preset positions
Site Monitoring
Routinely scan large areas such as parking lots, warehouses, and perimeters
Use preset tours for automated monitoring
Monitor multiple zones with a single PTZ camera
Emergency Response
Rapidly position camera on alarm location
Zoom in on emergency situations
Provide detailed view for security personnel
Best Practices
Preset Management
Use descriptive names such as "Front Entrance" or "Parking Lot North"
Assign logical numbers for quick access — 1–10 for critical positions
Regularly verify preset accuracy and adjust if needed
Movement Control
Use faster speed for large movements, slower for precision
Avoid excessive rapid movements to reduce wear on the camera mechanism
Use relative movement for fine adjustments after reaching a preset
Power Management
Avoid leaving camera in continuous pan mode unnecessarily
Configure camera to return to default position after inactivity
Use presets instead of manual movement when possible to reduce wear
Integration with Alarms
Configure camera to move to a preset when an alarm triggers
Create tours that visit alarm locations
Configure automatic PTZ movement based on events
Limitations and Considerations
Physical Limitations
Cameras have physical limits to pan and tilt range
Maximum movement speed varies by camera model
Digital zoom reduces image quality
Excessive use may reduce mechanism lifespan
Network Considerations
Network latency affects real-time control responsiveness
P2P Local Mode connections provide better PTZ responsiveness
Operational Considerations
Only one user should control PTZ at a time per camera
Multiple users changing presets simultaneously may cause conflicts
Appropriate user permissions required for PTZ

title: Video Streaming Configuration
excerpt:
This guide covers the configuration of video streaming for devices in GCXONE, including cloud and local mode setup, stream quality settings, and optimization options. Estimated time: 15–30 minutes per device type
Prerequisites Before configuring vide
content:
This guide covers the configuration of video streaming for devices in GCXONE, including cloud and local mode setup, stream quality settings, and optimization options. Estimated time: 15–30 minutes per device type
Prerequisites Before configuring video streaming, ensure the following conditions are met:
Devices are successfully added to GCXONE
Network connectivity is established
Device credentials are configured correctly
Required ports are open: RTSP (554), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443)
For Local Mode: Operator workstations are prepared with network access
Configuration Workflow The configuration process follows five main stages:
Device-Level Streaming Configuration
GCXONE Platform Streaming Settings
Local Mode Setup (Optional)
Stream Quality Optimization
Verification
Part 1: Device-Level Streaming Configuration
Step 1: Configure Device Streaming Settings Navigation: Device Management Interface → Video/Streaming Settings
Access the device's web interface or management software
Navigate to Video Settings or Streaming Configuration
Configure the Primary Stream:
Resolution: 1920x1080 or 1280x720 recommended
Frame Rate: 15–30 FPS for most use cases
Bitrate: 2–4 Mbps based on network capacity
Codec: H.264 or H.265 — H.265 recommended for better compression
Configure the Secondary Stream if available:
Resolution: Lower resolution such as 640x480
Bitrate: Lower bitrate for bandwidth optimization
Purpose: Used for multi-camera grid views
Enable RTSP Streaming if not already enabled
Set RTSP Port: Typically 554 or custom if required
Click Save or Apply
Note: Specific settings vary by device manufacturer. Refer to device documentation for exact navigation paths.
Step 2: Verify Network Connectivity
From GCXONE, verify the device shows Online status
Test RTSP stream connection using the device IP and port 554
Verify ports are accessible: RTSP (554), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443)
Check firewall rules allow required ports
Part 2: GCXONE Platform Streaming Configuration
Step 3: Configure Camera Streaming Settings in GCXONE Navigation: GCXONE → Devices → [Device Name] → Camera Configuration
Log into the GCXONE web portal
Navigate to Devices and select your device
Click Configure Cameras or Camera Management
For each camera, configure the following:
Cloud Streaming Settings
Enable Cloud Streaming for remote access
Stream Source: Primary or Secondary stream
Quality Mode: Auto, High, Medium, or Low
Enable Adaptive Quality for bandwidth optimization
Local Streaming Settings
Enable Local Streaming if using Local Mode
Stream Source: Primary stream recommended
Quality Mode: High — local network can handle higher quality
Advanced Streaming Options
Buffer Size: Adjust for network conditions
Timeout Settings: Increase if experiencing frequent timeouts
Retry Logic: Configure automatic reconnection attempts
Click Save Configuration
Step 4: Configure Global Streaming Preferences Navigation: GCXONE → Settings → Streaming Preferences
Configure Default Settings:
Default Quality: Auto recommended
Default Stream Source: Primary or Secondary
Concurrent Stream Limit: Maximum streams per user or workstation
Configure Bandwidth Management:

title: Video Streaming Overview
excerpt: Video Streaming Overview Video Streaming is one of the most critical capabilities within GCXONE, enabling operators to receive real-time, high-quality video from any connected camera or device — whether they're on-site, across the city, or on the oth
content:
Video Streaming Overview Video Streaming is one of the most critical capabilities within GCXONE, enabling operators to receive real-time, high-quality video from any connected camera or device — whether they're on-site, across the city, or on the other side of the world. GCXONE's streaming engine is built for professional-grade monitoring environments where reliability, latency, and clarity are non-negotiable.
Key Capabilities
Multi-Protocol Support GCXONE supports a broad range of streaming protocols to ensure compatibility with virtually any camera, NVR, or VMS on the market:
RTSP (Real-Time Streaming Protocol): The industry-standard protocol for IP cameras. Used for direct, low-latency video delivery from cameras and recorders.
P2P (Peer-to-Peer): Used in Local Mode to establish a direct connection between the operator's workstation and the device — eliminating cloud relay and dramatically reducing latency.
HLS (HTTP Live Streaming): Adaptive streaming protocol used for compatible devices such as Camect. Ideal for environments where standard RTSP is not available.
TCP JPEG / Raw: Used specifically for Milestone VMS integrations to ensure frame-accurate, high-fidelity video delivery.
Manufacturer SDKs: Native integration protocols for Milestone, Avigilon, Hikvision (HikProConnect), and Dahua (DoLynk) — enabling deeper feature support and optimized performance beyond what generic RTSP allows.
WebSocket Streaming: Used for real-time video delivery within web-based operator interfaces.
Low Bandwidth Mode: A specialized streaming mode designed for sites with constrained uplink capacity — reduces data consumption while maintaining a usable, responsive video feed.
Streaming Modes GCXONE supports two primary streaming architectures, each suited to different deployment scenarios: Cloud Streaming Video is routed through GCXONE's cloud infrastructure. This is the default mode and requires no special software installation on the operator's workstation. Cloud streaming provides:
Access from any location with an internet connection
End-to-end encryption for all video data in transit
Adaptive quality management to handle variable network conditions
Seamless multi-site access from a single interface Local Mode (P2P Streaming) When operators are physically on the same network as the cameras, Local Mode enables a direct peer-to-peer connection, bypassing the cloud entirely. This delivers:
Near-zero latency — critical for real-time alarm response
Reduced WAN bandwidth consumption, especially valuable at high-camera-count sites
Enhanced audio quality through native device SDK integration
Automatic fallback to cloud streaming if the local connection cannot be established
Local Mode requires the Local Mode service to be installed on the operator's workstation. See Local Mode Configuration for setup instructions. Stream Quality Management — GreenStream GCXONE's proprietary GreenStream technology is an intelligent adaptive streaming engine that automatically selects the optimal video resolution based on the size of the viewing window in the operator's interface. Rather than always requesting the highest available stream — which wastes bandwidth and strains CPU/GPU resources — GreenStream dynamically matches stream quality to what is actually visible on screen. Why it matters: An operator monitoring 16 cameras in a grid view does not need each feed at full 1080p. GreenStream detects the viewport size for each tile and requests the appropriate sub-stream, reducing total bandwidth and hardware load dramatically. Available quality settings:
Auto (GreenStream) — Recommended: Automatically adjusts resolution per-stream based on viewport dimensions, available bandwidth, and device capabilities. The best choice for most deployments.
High: Full-resolution stream. Best for single-camera full-screen viewing where maximum detail is needed.
Medium: Balanced quality and bandwidth. Suitable for multi-camera grid views.
Low: Optimized for bandwidth-constrained environments. Maintains a usable feed even on poor connections. GCXONE considers the following factors when determining adaptive quality: viewport size on screen, available network bandwidth, device hardware capabilities, connection type (cloud vs. local), and the number of concurrent streams open on the workstation.
How It Works
Cloud Streaming Architecture
The camera or NVR establishes and maintains a persistent connection to the GCXONE platform.
The operator opens a live view from the GCXONE interface.
GCXONE requests the video stream from the device using the appropriate protocol (RTSP, SDK, HLS, etc.).
The stream is transcoded and quality-optimized where required.
The video is securely delivered to the operator's browser or mobile app over an encrypted connection.
Local Mode Architecture
The Local Mode service is installed on the operator's workstation.
The service scans the local network and discovers connected devices.
A direct P2P connection is established between the workstation

title: ZenMode
excerpt: ZenMode is an intelligent operator-assist mode in GCXONE that optimizes how alerts are filtered, prioritized, and presented - so human operators can work faster, longer, and with less cognitive strain. In plain terms, ZenMode helps operators stay foc
content:
ZenMode is an intelligent operator-assist mode in GCXONE that optimizes how alerts are filtered, prioritized, and presented - so human operators can work faster, longer, and with less cognitive strain. In plain terms, ZenMode helps operators stay focused and calm under high alert volume by doing the heavy mental lifting for them. ZenMode is available as a premium feature through the GCXONE Marketplace. _Figure 1: ZenMode - operator-assist mode in the GCXONE Video Activity Search interface._
What ZenMode Does
Reduces cognitive load: Suppresses noise and highlights only high-confidence, relevant alarms so operators are never overwhelmed by false positives.
Speeds up decisions: Enables up to 3x faster alarm identification through smarter alert grouping and prioritization.
Improves capacity: Lets a single operator handle 200+ cameras without fatigue.
Boosts efficiency: Increases overall operator capacity by approximately 10% without adding staff.
Why It Matters In surveillance and security operations environments, fatigue leads to missed incidents. ZenMode is designed to prevent alert overload, maintain operator performance over long shifts, and improve response quality - without automation replacing human judgment. ZenMode filters out up to 60% of low-priority alarms, so operators stay sharp and focused on what actually matters. Alarm handling time is cut to 2-3 minutes, and a single operator can effectively manage 200+ cameras per shift.
Key Capabilities
Cuts noise at the source: Operators stop chasing junk alarms and spend time on events that matter, lowering fatigue and error rates.
Turns raw alarms into operational insight: Repeated alarms point to bad camera placement, wrong sensitivity, or environmental issues - improving detection quality across the site.
Reduces operating cost: Fewer false alarms mean fewer operator hours, fewer escalations, and fewer customer complaints.
Protects SLAs: By cleaning up the signal, ZenMode improves mean time to acknowledge and keeps contracts out of penalty territory.
Improves customer trust: When customers see fewer false alerts and faster, cleaner incident reports, confidence goes up - directly impacting renewals.
Shortens incident investigations: Site-wide historical footage loaded in one place means less back-and-forth and faster closure.
Strengthens compliance posture: ZenMode creates a clear audit trail for what happened, when, and what action was taken.
Scales teams without hiring: Better signal quality lets the same number of operators handle more cameras and more sites.
How It Works
Prerequisites Before activating ZenMode, ensure the following:
Administrator access to the GCXONE tenant is required.
Appropriate user permissions to activate marketplace features must be in place.
Historical alarm data and recorded footage must exist within the configured retention period for analysis to be effective.
Activating ZenMode ZenMode is activated through the GCXONE Marketplace. Follow these steps:
Log in to GCXONE as an administrator.
Navigate to Marketplace from the main menu.
Select Apps.
Find ZenMode and click Explore. Review the feature details. _Figure 2: ZenMode in Marketplace - Explore page with feature details and activation option._
Click Proceed.
When prompted to save the subscription, click Yes. After subscribing, ZenMode will appear under My Subscriptions and is immediately available for operators to use.
How to Access ZenMode
Navigate to Video Activity Search in GCXONE.
Choose a customer or site filter using the filters at the top of the screen.
Click the ZenMode icon from the toolbar in the top-right area of the screen. _Figure 3: Video Activity Search - ZenMode icon in the top-right toolbar._
Select the most important alarm to focus on.
ZenMode will compile the relevant events into a reviewable video.
Use the playback controls to navigate through the footage. _Figure 4: ZenMode compiled events - footage with playback controls for navigating between events._ _Figure 5: ZenMode event selection - camera event tiles with event counts for operator review._
Operator View When an operator selects a specific camera tile from the ZenMode event grid, the interface transitions into the Operator View - a focused investigation layout designed for in-depth analysis of a single event.
ZenMode event grid showing multiple camera feeds with event counts _Figure 6: ZenMode event grid - multiple camera feeds showing event counts per camera._
Layout Overview The Operator View presents a four-panel split-screen layout, giving the operator simultaneous context around the triggered event:
Live (top-left): Real-time feed from the camera at the current moment.
Post (top-right): Footage recorded immediately after the event trigger.
Pre (bottom-left): Footage recorded immediately before the event trigger.
Preview (bottom-right): Synchronized overview clip of the full event window. This quad-view allows operators to assess what happened before, during, and after an alarm without switch
