title: First-Time Login & Access
excerpt: Overview This section outlines the complete process for accessing GCXONE for the first time. It covers account registration, verification, password setup, and the initial login procedure. Each step must be completed in sequence to activate successful
content:
Overview This section outlines the complete process for accessing GCXONE for the first time. It covers account registration, verification, password setup, and the initial login procedure. Each step must be completed in sequence to activate successfully and access the platform.
Phase 1: Account Registration & Activation
Step 1: Access the Registration Portal Navigate to the GCXONE Portal (https://gcx.nxgen.cloud/) using a supported web browser (Chrome, Edge, or Firefox). Upon accessing the portal, the following elements are displayed:
A professional login interface
A prominent Register button for new users Select Register to begin the onboarding process. Step 2: Complete the Sign-Up Process The onboarding follows a guided three-stage wizard: Personal Info → Setup Cameras → Finishing Up Each stage must be completed before proceeding. Stage 1: Personal Information Enter the following details:
First Name — Account holder's first name
Last Name — Account holder's last name
Email — A valid business email address Select Continue to proceed. Stage 2: Setup Cameras Configure your tenant environment and select a deployment type. Tenant Configuration:
Tenant Name — Enter the official name for the tenant environment
Logo _(Optional)_ — Upload a company logo for branding Camera Selection — choose the option that aligns with your environment: Select Continue to proceed. Stage 3: Finishing Up The final stage summarizes the activation process.
Account Activation — Check your email for activation instructions
Platform Access — Access the dashboard after activation is completed Select Register to finalize the registration. Phase 2: Account Verification & Password Setup
Step 3: Email Verification After completing registration, a verification email is sent from: support@nxgen.io Open the email and select Confirm my account to activate your account.
️ Ensure emails from nxgen.io are not blocked by spam filters. If the verification email is not received:
Check spam or junk folders
Add nxgen.io to your email whitelist
Verify that corporate firewall policies are not blocking automated emails
Contact your IT department if filtering is suspected
Step 4: Password Creation After confirming your account, you will be redirected to the secure password setup page hosted by Auth0. Password Requirements:
Minimum 8 characters
At least 3 of the following: lowercase letters, uppercase letters, numbers, special characters (e.g. !@#$%^&*) Re-enter the password to confirm and select Reset password.
Step 5: Initial Login Return to the GCXONE portal and sign in using your newly created credentials.
Each user logs in with assigned credentials. Access is controlled based on role configuration — users only see features and entities relevant to their permissions.
Dashboard Overview After successful authentication, you are redirected to the GCXONE Dashboard — the central operational interface of the platform. The dashboard displays:
Total Alarms — overall alarm count across all sites
Alarms Passed — alarms processed and forwarded
Alarms Filtered Out — false alarms suppressed by NOVA99x
Unhealthy Cameras — devices flagged by HealthCheck
Checked Cameras / Healthy Cameras — camera health summary
Connection Failure — devices with connectivity issues
Real vs False Alarms — pie chart showing alarm accuracy ratio Additional interface elements:
Left-side navigation menu for core modules
Global search bar for quick navigation
Top-right controls: language selection, notifications, dark/light mode, and account options
Customer and site filters for scoped views
Time range selector for historical data
The dashboard adapts based on your role — operators see alarm widgets, managers see KPIs, and technicians see device health.

Accessing the Platform To access GCXONE for the first time, you will need the platform URL provided by your administrator, your registered email address, and the temporary password sent to your email.
Step 1: Navigate to the Login Page Open your web browser and navigate to the GCXONE platform URL. The login page will display the GCXONE branding with fields for email and password.
Step 2: Enter Your Credentials Enter your registered email address in the email field. Enter the temporary password provided in your welcome email. Click the "Sign In" button to proceed.
Step 3: Change Your Password On first login, you will be prompted to change your temporary password. Create a strong password following the security requirements displayed on screen. Confirm your new password and submit.
Step 4: Configure Two-Factor Authentication For enhanced security, enable two-factor authentication (2FA). You can use an authenticator app or receive codes via SMS. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete setup.
Step 5: Review Your Profile After completing security setup, review your user profile. Verify your contact information and notification preferences are correct. You are now ready to explore the platform.
Troubleshooting
If you did not receive the welcome email, check your spam folder
If your temporary password has expired, contact your administrator for a new one
If you are locked out after failed attempts, wait 15 minutes or contact support

title: Organization & Hierarchy Setup
excerpt:
Understanding the Hierarchy GCXONE uses a hierarchical structure to organize your monitoring infrastructure. The hierarchy consists of Organization, Regions, Clusters, Zones, and Sites. This structure enables efficient management and reporting.
Organ
content:
Understanding the Hierarchy GCXONE uses a hierarchical structure to organize your monitoring infrastructure. The hierarchy consists of Organization, Regions, Clusters, Zones, and Sites. This structure enables efficient management and reporting.
Organization Level The organization is the top level of your hierarchy. It represents your company or business unit. There is typically one organization per GCXONE deployment. Configure organization settings in the Admin panel.
Regions Regions represent geographic or operational divisions within your organization. Examples include North America, Europe, or East Coast. Create regions to group your monitoring infrastructure by location.
Clusters Clusters group sites within a region. A cluster might represent a city, district, or operational unit. Clusters help organize sites for reporting and management purposes.
Zones Zones further subdivide clusters into logical groupings. Use zones to categorize sites by type, customer, or operational criteria. Zones provide additional granularity for filtering and reporting.
Sites Sites are the monitoring locations where devices are installed. Each site contains the equipment being monitored. Sites can have associated addresses, contact information, and operational notes.
Creating the Hierarchy
Navigate to Admin > Organization Management
Create regions as needed for your operations
Add clusters within each region
Define zones within clusters
Add sites to the appropriate zone

title: Password Management & Security
excerpt:
Password Requirements GCXONE enforces strong password policies to protect your account. All passwords must meet the following minimum requirements:
Minimum 12 characters in length
At least one uppercase letter (A-Z)
At least one lowercase letter (a-z
content:
Password Requirements GCXONE enforces strong password policies to protect your account. All passwords must meet the following minimum requirements:
Minimum 12 characters in length
At least one uppercase letter (A-Z)
At least one lowercase letter (a-z)
At least one number (0-9)
At least one special character (!@#$%^&*)
Changing Your Password To change your password, navigate to your user profile and select "Change Password." Enter your current password, then enter and confirm your new password. Password changes take effect immediately.
Password Expiration Depending on your organization settings, passwords may expire after 90 days. You will receive a notification 14 days before expiration. Update your password before it expires to avoid account lockout.
Two-Factor Authentication Enable two-factor authentication (2FA) for an additional layer of security. GCXONE supports authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Microsoft Authenticator) and SMS verification.
Password Recovery If you forget your password, use the "Forgot Password" link on the login page. Enter your registered email address to receive a password reset link. The link expires after 24 hours.
Security Best Practices
Never share your password with others
Use a unique password for GCXONE
Consider using a password manager
Report suspicious activity immediately

title: Pre-Deployment Requirements
excerpt:
Overview Before beginning the GCXONE setup process, ensure that all required network, system, account, and hardware conditions are properly prepared. Completing these prerequisites ensures a smooth registration and deployment experience.
Network Requ
content:
Overview Before beginning the GCXONE setup process, ensure that all required network, system, account, and hardware conditions are properly prepared. Completing these prerequisites ensures a smooth registration and deployment experience.
Network Requirements
Internet Connection: Provide a stable broadband connection with a minimum speed of 10 Mbps.
IP Whitelisting: Ensure administrative access is available to configure firewall rules.
Port Availability: Ensure ports 80, 443, 554, and 123 are open on the firewall.
DNS Resolution : Confirm that the network can resolve and access the nxgen.cloud domain.
System Requirements
Operating System: Use Windows 10/11, macOS 10.15 or later, or Linux (Ubuntu 18.04 or later).
RAM: Minimum 8 GB (16 GB recommended for optimal performance).
Storage: Ensure at least 50 GB of available disk space for logs and temporary files.
Display: Use a display with a minimum resolution of 1920×1080 for proper dashboard visibility.
Account Requirements

title: Required Ports & Network Configuration
excerpt:
Network Requirements Overview GCXONE requires specific network configurations to function properly. This document outlines the ports and protocols needed for platform access, device communication, and third-party integrations.
Web Platform Access
HTT
content:
Network Requirements Overview GCXONE requires specific network configurations to function properly. This document outlines the ports and protocols needed for platform access, device communication, and third-party integrations.
Web Platform Access
HTTPS (443) - Required for secure web access to the platform
HTTP (80) - Redirects to HTTPS, optional but recommended
WebSocket (443) - Used for real-time updates and notifications
Device Communication
SNMP (161/UDP) - For SNMP-enabled monitoring devices
SNMP Trap (162/UDP) - For receiving SNMP trap alerts
Modbus TCP (502) - For Modbus-enabled equipment
MQTT (1883/8883) - For IoT device connectivity
API and Integration
REST API (443) - HTTPS endpoint for API calls
Webhooks (443) - Outbound webhook notifications
Firewall Configuration Configure your firewall to allow outbound connections to the GCXONE platform on ports 443 and 80. For on-premises deployments, ensure the necessary inbound ports are open for device communication.
Network Bandwidth Minimum recommended bandwidth is 10 Mbps for standard operations. Video streaming and large data exports may require additional bandwidth allocation.

content:
User Management System GCXONE provides a robust user management system that allows administrators to control access to the platform, assign roles, and manage permissions at various organizational levels.
Key Components
Users - Individual accounts with login credentials
Roles - Predefined permission sets for common job functions
Permissions - Granular access controls for specific features
Groups - Collections of users for easier management
Scopes - Geographic or organizational boundaries for access
Predefined Roles
Administrator - Full platform access and configuration rights
Operator - Monitoring, alarm handling, and basic reporting
Installer - Device setup, configuration, and commissioning
Integrator - API access and system integration features
Viewer - Read-only access to dashboards and reports
Access Scopes User access can be limited to specific parts of the organizational hierarchy:
Global - Access to all regions and sites
Region - Access to specific geographic regions
Cluster - Access to specific operational clusters
Zone - Access to specific zones within clusters
Site - Access to individual sites only
User Lifecycle
Invitation - Send welcome email with setup instructions
Activation - User completes first-time setup
Active - User has full access per their role and scope
Suspended - Temporary access restriction
Deactivated - Account disabled, data retained
Security Features
Password policies and expiration
Two-factor authentication (2FA)
Session management and timeout
Failed login lockout
Audit logging of all user actions
Getting Started To manage users, navigate to Admin > User Management. From here you can create new users, modify existing accounts, and review access permissions across your organization.

title: User Management Setup
excerpt: Overview GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments. info Key Concept Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entit
content:
Overview GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments. info Key Concept Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entity access determines WHICH customers, sites, and devices they can see. These are two separate things.
Step 1: Review Default Roles GCXONE provides predefined roles to simplify initial configuration: **** These roles can be used directly or customized depending on your operational requirements.
Step 2: Create or Modify Roles Navigate to Settings → Roles → Configure New Role The role creation wizard walks you through 4 steps:
Role Info — Name, description, and optional default role toggle
Users — Select which users belong to this role
Privileges — Enable or disable access per module (Dashboard, Video Viewer, Alarm Manager, Map, and more)
Entity Assignment — Choose which customers, sites, and devices users of this role can access Enable Include Children when selecting entities so any new devices or sensors added later are automatically accessible — no manual updates needed.
Step 3: Invite Users Navigate to Settings → Users and invite users by email. During the invitation process:
A role is assigned to the user
Module privileges are inherited from the assigned role
Entity access is applied based on role configuration After invitation, users receive an email to activate their account.
For further information, visit the Roles and Permissions page.

title: What is GCXOne
excerpt: What is GCXONE? GCXONE is a cloud-based, AI-powered monitoring and operations platform that delivers unified video surveillance and IoT services as a Security Management SaaS solution. It enables centralized, remote monitoring without requiring local
content:
What is GCXONE? GCXONE is a cloud-based, AI-powered monitoring and operations platform that delivers unified video surveillance and IoT services as a Security Management SaaS solution. It enables centralized, remote monitoring without requiring local infrastructure, providing a single intelligent interface for alarms, dashboards, device health, workflows, towers, and AI tools. GCXONE is not just a monitoring system — it is an operational control center designed for efficiency, scalability, and growth.
What Problem Does GCXONE Solve? Security operations often struggle with:
High volumes of false alarms (alarm fatigue)
Slow investigations and manual workflows
Cameras going offline unnoticed
Complex onboarding of customers and sites
Limited visibility across distributed locations GCXONE addresses these challenges using advanced AI analytics to significantly reduce false alarms and prioritize only critical events for operator review.
Core Operational Value
Reduce Alarm Fatigue Nova 99x leverages AI filtering to reduce false alarms dramatically and highlight real threats.
Improve Operational Efficiency Features like Z Mode (short compiled alarm summaries), Universal Search, and role-based customizable dashboards allow teams to investigate faster and operate smarter.
Increase Uptime Health Check proactively monitors device connectivity, black screen detection, obstruction detection, and low-light conditions. Issues are detected before they impact operations.
Enable Scalable Growth Bulk Import accelerates onboarding, Tower Guard centralizes mobile tower monitoring, and Marketplace enables instant feature activation.
How GCXONE Works Devices, VMS systems, and sensors send events to GCXONE. AI analyzes, filters, and classifies alarms. Verified alarms are prioritized for operator action. Alarms move through structured workflows including assignment, escalation, and reporting. This creates a traceable and intelligent operational lifecycle.
Integration with TALOS GCXONE works in coordination with Evalink TALOS as part of the operational workflow layer. Alarms are first analyzed and filtered within GCXONE. Only validated and prioritized alarms are forwarded to TALOS for structured operator handling and escalation. This significantly reduces manual verification workload and improves response efficiency.
Multi-Tenant Architecture & Platform Hierarchy GCXONE operates on a secure multi-tenant SaaS architecture that ensures data isolation per tenant, scalable customer onboarding, and secure remote accessibility. The platform follows a structured hierarchy: Tenant → Customer → Site → Device → Sensor This model ensures granular control, structured ownership, and permission-based visibility across distributed environments.
Multi-Level Operational Control GCXONE provides dashboards and operational control across:
Service Provider Level
Customer Level
Site Level
Device Level Each level includes reporting, monitoring, and management capabilities — ensuring structured oversight at every operational tier.
Role-Aware by Design GCXONE is role-aware by design. Each user sees only the modules, entities, and dashboards relevant to their responsibilities. Access is controlled through structured role configuration and entity permissions. This ensures operational clarity, security, and efficiency.
AI at the Core GCXONE includes Genie, an embedded AI assistant that:
Answers operational questions
Provides analytics insights
Assists with troubleshooting
Integrates with ticketing and workflows Users can ask instead of navigating complex menus.
In One Sentence GCXONE is not only monitoring — it is an AI-powered operations and growth platform.

title: Bandwidth Requirements
excerpt:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to
content:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to estimate the total bandwidth required for your installation:
Total Bandwidth =
(Number of Cameras × Avg Bitrate) + (Concurrent Viewers × Streaming Bitrate) + 30% Management Overhead
Bitrate Guidelines Bandwidth consumption varies significantly based on resolution and the codec used. H.265 (HEVC) is highly recommended for 50% bandwidth savings. Installation Examples
Small (10 Cameras)
Resolution: 1080p (H.265)
Upload Required: ~30 Mbps
Viewers: 2 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 50 Mbps Symmetric
Medium (50 Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed 1080p/4MP (H.265)
Upload Required: ~200 Mbps
Viewers: 10 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 400 Mbps Symmetric
Large (200+ Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed (H.265)
Upload Required: ~1 Gbps
Total Recommended: 2 Gbps Dedicated Link
Optimization Strategies
Use H.265 Everywhere: Ensure your cameras and NVRs are set to HEVC/H.265 to cut data costs.
Motion-Based Bitrate: Configure Variable Bitrate (VBR) so the stream only spikes during actual activity.
Sub-Streaming: Use a lower resolution "Sub-Stream" for multi-camera grid views, and only switch to "Main Stream" for full-screen inspection.
VLAN Segmentation: Keep video traffic on a dedicated VLAN to prevent congestion with general office data.
Common Issues
Freezing/Artifacts: Usually caused by sustained upload congestion. Try reducing the frame rate from 30 fps to 15 fps.
Delayed Alarms: Often a result of network saturation. Implement Quality of Service (QoS) to prioritize alarm handshake traffic (Port 443).

title: Browser Errors
excerpt:
Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
The Root Cause
content:
Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
The Root Cause Talos is an embedded application. It runs inside the Genesis interface but communicates with a separate security backend. To maintain your session across these two domains, your browser must allow third-party cookies.
️ How to Fix: Quick Toggle The fastest way to fix this is using the address bar toggles:
Look at the right side of your Browser Address Bar.
Click the Eye icon (️) or Cookie icon.
Toggle the switch to "Allow Third-Party Cookies" for this site.
Refresh the page.
️ Manual Browser Settings If the quick toggle isn't visible, use the manual settings for your specific browser:
Go to Settings → Privacy and Security → Third-party cookies. 2. Select Allow third-party cookies. 3. Alternatively: Add [.]nxgen.cloud and [.]evalink.io to the "Allowed to use third-party cookies" list. 1. Go to Settings → Cookies and site permissions. 2. Ensure "Allow sites to save and read cookie data" is On. 3. Ensure "Block third-party cookies" is Off. 1. Go to Settings (or Preferences) → Privacy. 2. Uncheck "Prevent cross-site tracking". 3. Uncheck "Block all cookies". Troubleshooting Checklist
Incognito Mode: If Talos works in Incognito, clear your regular browser cache.
Ad-Blockers: Some ad-blockers (like uBlock Origin) treat Talos cookies as "trackers." Whitelist the site in your extension settings.
Corporate VPN: Some VPNs strip cookie headers. Try connecting from a direct internet line to rule out network-level blocking.

title: Image & Video Display Issues
excerpt:
️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video Activ
content:
️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video Activity buffer shows empty image slots, verify the following:
Check Time Synchronization Discrepancies between the device clock and GCXONE (even 5-10 seconds) are the primary cause of image retrieval failure.
Ensure the device is using time1.nxgen.cloud or pool.ntp.org.
DST Check: Ensure Daylight Saving Time is toggled ON. Without this, the system may fail during biannual time shifts.
Verify Recording Duration Short recording buffers can cause retrieval misses.
Recommended Setting: Configure your device to record at least 30 seconds of video per event (not 10 seconds). This provides enough "padding" to capture three reliable snapshots even if clocks are slightly out of sync.
Storage Health (HDD Failure) Check the device dashboard for HDD Failure or Disk Full alerts.
If the hard drive is failing, the device will stop buffering video, and all subsequent alarms will have "Empty" image evidence.
Incorrect/Mixed Alarm Images In rare cases, an alarm from "Site A" may display an image from "Site B." This is usually a symptom of Central Cache Corruption.
Scope Check: Determine if the issue affects a single camera or the entire site.
Root Cause: If images are routinely swapped across different physical NVRs, the local metadata index or the cloud cache index is likely out of sync.
Resolution: Document the affected sites and escalate to the Technical Systems Team for a cache-flush operation.
Streaming Failures (Local Mode) If "Cloud Mode" works but "Local Mode" fails to load live video:
H.265 vs H.264 GCXONE cloud mode handles H.265 transcoding server-side. However, Local Mode sends raw packets directly from the Edge device to your browser.
Check Encoding: Verify if the camera is set to H.265.
Install Codec: Ensure your Windows operating system has the H.265 video extensions installed.
Switch to H.264: If the client machine cannot be updated, switch the camera's sub-stream encoding to H.264 for maximum compatibility.
IP Camera Limitations Independent IP cameras have limited processing power and often restrict the number of concurrent RTSP connections.
If multiple operators are viewing the same IP camera, the device may refuse further connections.
Solution: Switch the device to Cloud Only Mode. This routes all requests through a single cloud bridge, bypassing the device's connection limit.
Quick Diagnostics Table Symptom Probable Cause Immediate Action Blue/Black Screen H.265 Codec Missing Install extensions or switch to H.264. "No Image" Button Time Sync Drifting Re-sync device via NTP. Site A shows Site B Cache Corruption Escalate to Tech Team. Laggy/Stuttering Low Upload Bandwidth Reduce sub-stream bitrate/FPS.

title: Platform Architecture Overview
excerpt:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:
content:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:

title: Alarm Flow
excerpt:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm i
content:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm is triggered when one of the following occurs: A camera or sensor detects a real security event that passes Nova 99x AI filtering. A Health Check failure is detected, such as a camera going offline, obstruction, black screen, or low light condition. A manual alarm is triggered by an operator or system administrator.
Alarm Delivery Confirmed alarms are delivered to the operator queue in Talos in real time. Each alarm arrives with full context including the customer and site name, device and sensor reference, event timestamp, alarm classification, and a GIF preview or video clip for fast review.
Operator Queue Inside Talos, operators see a live queue of incoming alarms. Each alarm in the queue can be assigned to a specific operator, preventing duplicate handling. The queue displays who is currently working on each alarm, making team coordination visible at a glance.
Alarm Review The operator opens the alarm and reviews the available information: GIF Preview: A short animated preview of the event for instant context. Jump Back: Replay the 30 seconds before the alarm was triggered. Live View: Switch to a live stream of the camera in real time. Playback: Review recorded footage around the event. Area Masking: Apply a temporary or permanent mask to a specific zone if needed.
Alarm Actions After reviewing the alarm, the operator takes one of the following actions: Confirm as Real: Escalate the alarm and trigger the configured workflow — such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or contacting the customer. Mark as False: Close the alarm as a false positive. This data feeds back into Nova 99x analytics. Escalate: Assign the alarm to a supervisor or higher-level operator.
Workflow Automation GCXONE integrates with Talos workflows to automate alarm response. Depending on the alarm type and site configuration, workflows can automatically notify the customer, dispatch a technician, send an SMS or email alert, escalate to a supervisor, or log the response for SLA reporting.
Alarm Resolution & Audit Every alarm is closed with a resolution note and stored in the Audit Log with full details including the operator who handled it, the time of action, the steps taken, and the final resolution. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits.

title: Cloud Architecture
excerpt:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the compl
content:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the complexity of traditional on-premise systems. By shifting to a SaaS model, organizations gain agility, scalability, and freedom from hardware maintenance. No Local Servers: Eliminates dedicated local NVR/DVR maintenance. Infinite Scalability: Add sites and cameras instantly without hitting physical hardware limits. Centralized Management: Deploy security patches and features globally in seconds.
Infrastructure Stack (AWS) The platform is hosted on Amazon Web Services (AWS) using a containerized microservices architecture managed by Kubernetes (EKS). Compute & Orchestration Private EKS Clusters: Core application logic runs on secure, private worker nodes. Auto-Scaling: Node groups adjust resources in real-time based on traffic and processing load. Messaging: Uses Amazon MQ for asynchronous event synchronization across the global platform. Storage & Data Asset Storage: Snapshots and icons are stored in encrypted S3 Buckets. Metadata Management: Uses scalable NoSQL databases for lightning-fast camera metadata retrieval.
The Universal Translator (Proxy Layer) The Proxy Architecture is GCXONE's core innovation. It allows the platform to communicate with diverse hardware manufacturers — Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, and others — by translating their proprietary protocols into a standardized unified command set. The flow works as follows: Diverse hardware communicates via SDK or ONVIF to the Proxy Layer, which acts as a translation engine. The Proxy Layer converts this into a standardized stream using WebRTC or RTSP, which then feeds into the GCXONE Core for AI processing and display. The Proxy Layer removes the need for expensive hardware bridges. Your cameras talk directly to the cloud via the GCXONE gateway.
Multi-Tenant Logical Hierarchy Data isolation is enforced through a strict multi-tenant model. Each organization lives in a dedicated Tenant space, ensuring data privacy and granular access control. The hierarchy is structured as follows: Tenant: The monitoring station or master organization. Customer: Individual client organizations under the tenant. Site: Physical facilities such as offices or warehouses. Device: NVRs or Gateways located at the site. Sensor: Individual camera channels or PIR sensors connected to a device.
Security & Compliance Security is integrated at the architectural level, not added as a bolt-on feature. Encryption: Data at rest and in transit is secured with AES-256. Access Gating: Jump Servers and private VPCs isolate infrastructure from the public web. Authentication: Integrated with Auth0 for enterprise-grade MFA and SSO. Continuous Audit: Every system call and operator action is logged in an immutable audit trail.

title: Event Processing
excerpt: Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the poin
content:
Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the point an operator takes action.
Event Lifecycle Every event in GCXONE follows a defined lifecycle: Detection: A camera or sensor detects an activity and sends a signal to the GCXONE platform via the Proxy Layer. Ingestion: The platform receives the raw event and logs it with a timestamp, device ID, site, and customer reference. AI Analysis: The event is passed through the Nova 99x AI engine, which classifies it as a Real Alarm, False Alarm, or Technical Event. Routing: Confirmed real alarms are routed to the operator queue via Talos for action. Resolution: The operator reviews, processes, and closes the event. All actions are logged in the Audit Trail.
Event Types GCXONE classifies every incoming event into one of three categories: Real Alarm: A genuine security event that requires operator attention, such as motion detection, line crossing, or intrusion. False Alarm: An event triggered by non-threatening activity such as animals, weather, or lighting changes. Filtered out by Nova 99x to reduce operator workload. Technical Event: A system-generated event triggered by Health Check, such as a camera going offline, a black screen, obstruction detection, or low light conditions.
AI Processing — Nova 99x Before any event reaches an operator, it passes through Nova 99x — GCXONE's AI filtering engine. Nova 99x analyzes each event using behavioral analysis and image recognition to determine whether it represents a real threat. This process reduces false alarm volume by up to 99%, freeing operators to focus only on events that matter. Filtering can be applied across thermal and optical image types.
Event Routing Once classified, events are routed based on their type and priority: Real Alarms are pushed to the operator queue in Talos, where they can be assigned, reviewed, and actioned according to configured workflows. False Alarms are filtered out and stored for reporting and analytics. They do not reach the operator queue. Technical Events trigger automated notifications and can initiate workflows such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or escalating to a supervisor.
Event States Every event moves through the following states during its lifecycle: New: Event received and pending review. Filtered: Classified as a false alarm by Nova 99x and removed from the operator queue. Assigned: Allocated to a specific operator for action. In Progress: Operator is actively reviewing the event. Processed: Event has been reviewed and closed.
Event Data & Audit Every event is stored with full metadata including timestamp, device and sensor reference, site and customer, event type and classification, operator actions, and resolution notes. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits through the Audit Log.

title: GCXONE & Talos Interaction
excerpt: GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and s
content:
GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and synchronize site metadata across the ecosystem.
Automatic Site Synchronization When an administrator creates a site in GCXONE, the platform automatically generates a corresponding record in Talos via MQTT messaging. Bidirectional Sync: Updates to site names, addresses, or contact details in GCXONE are pushed to Talos in real time. Test Mode Intelligence: If a site in Talos is set to Test Mode during maintenance, GCXONE automatically disarms that site to prevent unnecessary AI processing.
The Alarm Lifecycle The most critical interaction between GCXONE and Talos occurs during a security event. The flow works as follows: Detection: A hardware device detects a breach and sends a signal to Talos. Forwarding: Talos uses a pre-configured workflow to forward images or video clips to the GCXONE AI engine. Verification: GCXONE's AI classifies the event based on configured priority, white, and black lists. Non-threatening movements such as shadows or animals are filtered out. Response: The operator receives a verified alarm in Talos, complete with bounding boxes showing exactly where the threat was detected. GCXONE's AI reduces false alarms by approximately 80%, ensuring operators focus only on genuine security threats.
Key Automation Workflows Arm/Disarm Schedules: GCXONE can automate the arming and disarming of sites based on client business hours. When the schedule reaches the arming time, GCXONE calls the device API and waits 30 seconds for a status confirmation. If the device fails to arm, an Arming Failure alert is sent to the operator. Event Overflow Protection: To prevent system overload from faulty sensors, GCXONE implements overflow protection. If 25 or more alarms are received from a single sensor within 5 minutes, alarms are temporarily discarded and a maintenance ticket is generated automatically.

title: Getting to Know Talos
excerpt:
Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts.
Architecture & Reliability Talos is built
content:
Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts.
Architecture & Reliability Talos is built on a cloud-native microservices architecture. Every feature — from alarm processing to site management — runs as an independent service on Amazon Web Services (AWS). Global Redundancy: Servers are distributed across America, Europe, and Asia. Fail-Safe Processing: If a specific microservice encounters an issue, the rest of the platform continues to operate without interruption. Silent Updates: Security patches and feature releases are deployed remotely. When a major change occurs, you will see a prompt to reload the page to apply the update immediately.
Navigating the Dashboard When you log in, you are greeted with the Main Dashboard providing a high-level overview of your security health. Alarm Queue: Real-time view of incoming alerts. My Assigned Alarms: Tracks incidents currently under your review. What's New: Click your Profile Icon then What's New to see the latest feature releases and security patches.
Understanding Sites in Talos In Talos, a Site is any entity that can generate an alarm — a building, a specific floor, or even a personal panic button. Each site has one of three statuses: Active: Fully operational. Alarms are processed and recorded. Inactive: No alarms are accepted. Inactive sites do not count toward billing. Test Mode: Used during maintenance. Motion alerts can be ignored while keeping fire alarms active.
Organizing Your Workspace As your organization grows, use Talos grouping features to keep data structured: Organizational Units: The highest level of grouping, such as a regional division. Site Groups: Regional or functional clusters, such as a group of retail locations in a specific area. Service Companies: Link external technicians or guard services to specific sites for automated task routing.
Creating a Site in Talos Go to the Sites page and click Create Site. Choose an ID method — enter a descriptive name manually such as London HQ, or click Sequence to have Talos generate a unique numeric ID automatically. This is useful for large deployments. Scroll to the Address pane and enter the physical location. Ensure the address matches what is configured in GCXONE for accurate synchronization. Optionally add custom fields such as License Plate Number or Site Manager Cell to use in automated workflows.
Virtual Devices & Connections The Virtual Devices tab lists all receivers and connection points in the Talos cloud. Green Icon: Healthy connection. Red Icon: Disconnected hardware. Dual Path (DP): High-security devices using both Ethernet and mobile or cellular backups for redundancy.

title: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model
excerpt: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration
content:
Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration and configuration.
The 5-Level Hierarchy Structure The hierarchy flows from top to bottom as follows: Tenant → Customer → Site → Device → Camera/Sensor. Each level is nested inside the one above it, creating a clean organizational structure that enforces data isolation at every layer.
Level 1: Tenant The Tenant is the top-level organization with completely isolated data and configuration. Complete Data Isolation: Each tenant operates in its own dedicated space. Independent Configuration: Custom settings, branding, and integrations per tenant. Subdomain Access: Each tenant gets a unique subdomain such as company.nxgen.cloud. Multi-Customer Support: A single tenant can serve multiple customers, making it ideal for the MSSP model. Common use cases include Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs), large enterprises with multiple divisions, and service providers offering white-label solutions.
Level 2: Customer The Customer represents individual clients or sub-organizations within a tenant. Client Organization: A distinct organization or business unit under the tenant. Isolated Data: Customer data is completely separate from other customers. Custom Configuration: Site-specific settings and device configurations per customer. User Management: Customer-level user accounts and permissions. Common use cases include individual businesses served by an MSSP, different divisions within a large enterprise, and separate legal entities under a parent organization.
Level 3: Site A Site is a physical location such as a building, facility, or geographic area. Physical Location: Includes a real-world address and geographic coordinates. Talos Synchronization: Sites automatically sync with Talos CMS via MQTT messaging. Site names, addresses, contact info, coordinates, and configuration parameters are kept consistent in real time with bidirectional synchronization and error handling. Device Grouping: All devices at a location are grouped under their site. Address Mapping: Used for mapping, routing, and location services. Note: To delete a site, you must be at the Customer level.
Level 4: Device A Device is the security hardware at a site — such as an NVR, DVR, alarm panel, or IoT gateway. Device types supported: NVR (IP camera recording), DVR (analog camera recording), Alarm Panels (intrusion detection), and IoT Gateways (smart sensors). Each device requires the following configuration: a unique Server Unit ID, IP address and port, authentication credentials, and NTP configuration. Important: Each device must have a unique Server Unit ID. Duplicate IDs cause alarm attribution conflicts.
Level 5: Camera / Sensor The Camera or Sensor is the individual channel connected to a device. Sensor types include video cameras, motion sensors, door sensors, and IoT sensors. Individual Channel: Each sensor occupies a separate channel on its parent device. Automatic Discovery: Sensors are discovered automatically when a device is added. Alarm Attribution: Each sensor can independently trigger alarms and events. Capabilities per sensor may include live stream, playback, PTZ control, and analytics such as motion detection and line crossing.
Common Hierarchy Patterns Single Organization: One tenant contains one customer, which contains one or more sites. Multi-Location Business: One tenant contains one customer with multiple sites — ideal for retail chains or enterprises with branch offices. MSSP Model: One tenant contains multiple customers, each with their own sites and devices — the standard model for managed security service providers.
Integration with Talos CMS GCXONE and Talos are automatically synchronized by default. Sites created in GCXONE appear directly in Talos. Alarms are attributed to the correct site and device, and events are correlated based on hierarchy relationships. If a specific site is not fully synced, you can manually force synchronization from the site actions menu using Push to Talos (sends data from GCXONE to Talos) or Pull from Talos (retrieves data from Talos to GCXONE).
Best Practices Use clear, consistent naming conventions across all levels. Ensure site addresses are accurate for Talos synchronization. Always assign unique Server Unit IDs to avoid alarm attribution conflicts. Organize sites and devices logically to reflect real-world structure. Document tenant-specific configurations for future reference. Avoid reusing Server Unit IDs, using vague names, skipping address validation, or ignoring hierarchy when setting permissions.

Inviting Users
Onboarding new team members is handled through an automated email workflow. This ensures that every user securely verifies their identity and sets their own private credentials.
Step 1: Navigate to Users
Open Settings
Click on Users
Click Invite New User to start adding a new user
Step 2: Enter User Information Fill in the required fields: Personal Information:
First Name
Last Name
Email Address (required — serves as login ID and must be unique)
Phone Number (optional — for SMS alerts) Address Information (optional):
Street Name
Building Number
Zip Code
City
Country
Step 3: Configure Account Settings Role Assignment: Select the role that defines what this user can access, such as End User, Admin, or Operator. Each role determines which features and actions the user can access. Session Timeout: Set the session timeout period. Default is 30 minutes. Range is 30 to 1440 minutes (0.5 to 24 hours).
Step 4: Send Invitation Click Submit. The user will receive two automated emails:
Email Confirmation: Verifies the email is active.
Password Setup Link: Allows the user to create their secure password.
What Happens Next? Once you click Submit, the account setup process works as follows:
The user receives an email titled "Changing your password for the NXGEN application."
They click the password reset link in the email.
They create a new password for their account.
Upon first login, they are directed to the appropriate section based on their role. If Dashboard is enabled in their role, they land on the Dashboard. If Dashboard is not enabled, they land on the first accessible section such as Configuration.
Multi-Organization Access Users invited to multiple tenants use the same email address. Single Identity: They only need one password. Tenant Selection: Upon login, they will be prompted to choose which organization they want to access for that session. Switching: They can switch between tenants anytime via Settings → Switch Tenant.
Troubleshooting Password Setup Link Expired: For security, the password setup link expires after 24 hours. If a user misses this window, you must Resend Invitation from the Users table. Email Not Received: Ask the user to check their Spam or Junk folder for mail from @nxgen.cloud. Login Issues: Ensure the user clicked the Verification Link in the first email before attempting to use the password setup link.

Managing Users Once users are onboarded, administrators can modify their permissions and client visibility as their responsibilities evolve.
Viewing and Searching Users
Go to Settings → Users.
Search Bar: Quickly find a user by their name or email.
Editing User Access If a team member changes roles or needs to monitor new clients:
Go to Settings → Users.
Locate the user in the User List table.
Click the Actions (···) menu next to the user.
You will see three options:
Edit User — Update personal information, role, customer group, or session timeout.
Edit User Entities — Assign or modify which customers and sites this user can access using the entity tree with Override or Merge mode.
Remove User — Permanently remove the user from the platform.
Select Edit User to update the following:
First Name and Last Name
Email Address
Phone Number
Address Information
Genesis Role and GCXONE Role
Customer Group
Session Timeout
Click Update User to save changes. To update entity access specifically, select Edit User Entities from the Actions menu. This opens the entity tree where you can assign customers and sites to the user using Override or Merge mode. Permission changes take effect immediately. The user may need to refresh their browser tab to see new sidebar icons or client sites.
Remove User When a user no longer needs access, locate the user in the User List table, click the Actions (···) menu, and select Remove User. Removing a user permanently removes their record from the platform. The account cannot be restored after deletion. Note: Use Remove User only when you are certain the user no longer needs access. For temporary access removal, consider updating their role to End User or restricting their entity access instead.
Security Best Practices Quarterly Audits: Review your user list every 3 months. Remove or restrict access for anyone who hasn't logged in recently. Offboarding: Remove user access the moment a team member leaves the organization. Avoid Shared Accounts: Never share a single login between multiple people. This destroys your audit trail.

The Pain Points "I have to jump between multiple pages to see what access a role actually grants" "Creating a new role takes forever with too man
content:
BY NXGEN
Role-Based Access Control Administrator Guide GCXONE Security Management Platform February 2026
The Pain Points "I have to jump between multiple pages to see what access a role actually grants" "Creating a new role takes forever with too many configuration steps" "I can’t quickly tell what permissions my users have" "Two users in the same role need access to different customers, but I have to create separate roles for each"
What’s Different Now Guided role wizard – A step-by-step flow walks you through Role Information, User Assignment, Module Privileges, and Entity Access in a single process Per-module privileges – Control access at the module level (Dashboard, Configuration, Video Activity Search, Marketplace, Video Viewer, Alarm Manager, Map) with granular capability toggles within each module Dual-level entity access – Entity access can now be configured both at the role level AND at the individual user level. Two users can share the same role and privileges while accessing completely different customers or sites Include Children toggle – When selecting entities in the tree, toggle Include Children to automatically include all current AND future sub-entities (devices, sensors) under a selected parent User assignment during role creation – Select which users belong to a role directly within the role creation wizard Edit Entity Access – A dedicated interface for managing per-user entity access with Override and Merge modes, accessible directly from the Role Management page Entity Groups – Save named collections of customers, sites, devices, and sensors as reusable templates. Assign an Entity Group to a role or individual user instead of manually picking entities each time Default role toggle – Mark a role as the default so new users are automatically assigned to it upon invitation Key Distinction: Entity Access vs. Privileges Entity access and privileges are separate concepts in GCXONE. Privileges (configured in the role) determine WHAT actions a user can perform. Entity access determines WHICH customers, sites, devices, and sensors they can see. User-level customization is done through entity access only — there are no user-level privilege overrides.
What Stayed the Same You don’t need to relearn everything: User management works exactly as before User groups still function the same way The entity hierarchy (Service Provider → Customer → Site → Device → Sensor) remains unchanged
How It Works A role is the core unit of access control in GCXONE. Each role combines three elements:
Role Information & Users Name, description, default toggle, assigned users
Module Privileges Which modules & capabilities users can access
Entity Assignment Which customers, sites, devices, sensors to access Role Information & Users: Basic role details (name, description, default role toggle) and the users assigned to this role. Module Privileges: Which platform modules users can access, and which specific capabilities are enabled within each. Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entity Assignments: Which customers, sites, devices, and sensors users can access. Entity access determines WHERE a user can operate. Entity assignments can be configured at two levels: Role-Level Entities: Entities assigned within the role itself, shared by all users in the role User-Level Entities: Entities assigned to individual users through Edit Entity Access, allowing different users in the same role to access different customers or sites Tip Previously, if two users needed the same privileges (e.g., Admin access) but managed different customers, you had to create two separate roles. Now, create one role and customize entity access per user through Edit Entity Access.
Default Roles GCXONE includes pre-configured roles to help you get started quickly. Each role comes with a defined set of module privileges. Entity access can be further customized per user. Role Description Key Capabilities Super Admin Full platform access with complete control over all modules, users, roles, and settings. Complete administrative access to all system features. Can manage all users, roles, and permissions. Full access to all entities. Configure system-wide settings and audit logs. One Super Admin per tenant. Cannot be deleted. Admin Administrative access — can manage users, roles, configuration, and reports, but not billing. Manage users and role assignments. Configure system settings. Create and manage Entity Groups. View audit logs and reports. Cannot access billing. Cannot delete critical system components. Operator Operational access — can view and manage configuration and reports but cannot delete or administer users/roles. Day-to-day monitoring and alarm processing. View dashboards and configuration. Generate and view reports. Handle operational tasks. Cannot delete system components. Limited administrative capabilities. Installer Field technician access — can manage devices, sensors, and network configuration. Manage physical devices and sensors. Co

title: Site Synchronization
excerpt: Site Synchronization GCXONE and Talos are designed to work as a unified system. Site Synchronization ensures that all site data created or updated in GCXONE is automatically reflected in Talos, and vice versa — keeping both platforms consistently ali
content:
Site Synchronization GCXONE and Talos are designed to work as a unified system. Site Synchronization ensures that all site data created or updated in GCXONE is automatically reflected in Talos, and vice versa — keeping both platforms consistently aligned without manual intervention.
How It Works By default, GCXONE and Talos synchronize automatically. When a new site is created in GCXONE, it appears directly in Talos. The following data is synchronized between the two platforms: Site name and address, contact information, geographic coordinates, configuration parameters, and operational status. Synchronization happens in real time via MQTT messaging, with built-in error handling and retry logic to ensure reliability.
Manual Synchronization If a specific site is not fully synced, you can force synchronization manually from the site actions menu: Push to Talos: Sends the latest site data from GCXONE to Talos. Pull from Talos: Retrieves and updates site data from Talos into GCXONE.
How to Manually Sync a Site Go to Configuration → Customers and select the relevant customer. Open the Sites tab and locate the site. Click the actions menu (···) next to the site. Select Push to Talos or Pull from Talos depending on the direction needed.
Arm & Disarm From the same actions menu, you can also arm or disarm a site directly without navigating to Talos. This is useful for quick operational control during incidents or maintenance windows.
When to Use Manual Sync Manual synchronization is needed when a site was recently created but has not yet appeared in Talos, when site details were updated in one platform but not reflected in the other, or when troubleshooting synchronization issues between the two platforms.

title: Talos User Management
excerpt: Talos User Management While GCXONE manages the account identity, Talos handles the behavioral settings of how an operator interacts with alarms. For more detailed information about Talos user management, visit the Evalink Documentation at documentati
content:
Talos User Management While GCXONE manages the account identity, Talos handles the behavioral settings of how an operator interacts with alarms. For more detailed information about Talos user management, visit the Evalink Documentation at documentation.evalink.io
Users Overview Users are company members and other individuals who can work in Talos under the Company account. Talos operates the following user-related terms:
User Roles
User Types
User Profiles Only Administrators can invite and manage users, create new User Roles and User Types.
User Roles Each user has a role that defines their level of permissions. There are four preset User Roles in Talos. They cannot be changed or deleted. Administrators can also create custom User Roles based on the preset User Roles. A custom User Role allows you to configure a custom set of permissions. You can view all User Roles for your company under Company → Settings → Users. The following preset User Roles exist in Talos: Administrator Has a full set of permissions. There can be several Administrators under one Company. Manager Intended for managing the work of users with Operator and Operator Minimal User Roles. Like Administrator, Manager can create and manage major Talos objects such as workflows, sites, site groups, and schedules, but access to some features is restricted. For example, Manager cannot invite users or edit global alarm settings. Operator Intended for alarm processing. Operator has access to all Talos interface pages but for the most part cannot create or manage objects — only has permissions for viewing. Operator Minimal Also intended for alarm processing. Compared to Operator, Operator Minimal has access to a limited number of interface pages. Both Operator and Operator Minimal can create and edit certain Talos objects if required to do so by a workflow — for example, if the Add Schedule Entry step is included in a manual workflow.
User Types User Types allow fine-tuning of user experience by combining User Roles with certain pre-configured work environments such as Test Types and Workspaces, and access permissions such as selected access to Site Groups in your company. User Types narrow down the flow of incoming alarms for each user in order to more effectively distribute alarms between available operators. There are four preset User Types in Talos. They cannot be deleted, but Administrators can change their settings. Administrators can also add and delete custom User Types. The following preset User Types exist in Talos: Administrator Default User Role: Administrator. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Manager Default User Role: Manager. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Operator Default User Role: Operator. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Operator Minimal Default User Role: Operator Minimal. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Note: User Role is a kernel setting that defines available user permissions. User Type is the main user-defining quality — it combines a User Role and available working environments (Workspaces and Test Types). When Administrators invite new users, they select a User Type for them when issuing an invite.
User Profiles A User Profile is the Talos user interface page where you can view all your user information and manage your user activity. To access your user profile, click the user profile icon in the upper right corner and select Profile from the dropdown menu. The following tabs are available on the User Profile page: Profile: View all your user details, monitor and manage your user activity and sessions, and perform basic user actions. Company Management: View which companies you are a member of, accept invites, and switch between companies without logging out and back in.
How Users Are Created To create a new user, an Administrator sends an invite to the user. The only prerequisite required is a valid email address. The user then logs in to Talos following the instructions in the invitation and verification emails. After the first user login, the record of the user appears in the list on Company → Users. When inviting a user, the Administrator selects a User Type from the list of available types which includes preset and custom User Types. After a user accepts the invite and logs in, Administrators can change their User Type.
Managing Your User Profile
Change User Name
In the Profile tab of the User Profile page, scroll to Profile Actions and select Change Name.
Type your First Name and Last Name in the corresponding fields. Both fields are required. You must enter at least 3 characters for each.
Click Let's Start. Talos will log you out for the changes to take effect. Log back in to continue.
Change User Password
In the Profile tab, scroll to Profile Actions and select Change Password. Talos sends an email to your login address.
Follow the link in the email.
Enter your new password in both fields so they match. Your password must be

title: Talos Workflows
excerpt:
Talos Workflows Workflows are the brain of Talos. They define exactly how an incoming alarm should be handled — whether it's automatically closing a test signal or guiding an operator through a high-priority burglary response.
How Workflow Priority W
content:
Talos Workflows Workflows are the brain of Talos. They define exactly how an incoming alarm should be handled — whether it's automatically closing a test signal or guiding an operator through a high-priority burglary response.
How Workflow Priority Works An alarm is always processed for a specific site. Talos searches for a matching workflow in the following priority order: Site Level: The most specific — workflows configured directly for a single site take highest priority. Site Group: Workflows applied to a group of sites. Global/Company Level: The default fallback workflow applied when no site-level or group-level workflow matches.
Creating a Workflow Step 1 — Define Incoming Conditions: Set the triggers that activate the workflow. This includes the alarm code such as BA for Burglary, the schedule such as After Hours only, and the logic — use All of (AND) for strict matching or One of (OR) for broad categories. Step 2 — Add Decision Nodes: Ask the system or the operator a question. Examples include automated checks such as whether the site is currently armed, or manual checks such as whether video shows a human presence. Step 3 — Configure Actions: Define what happens next. Options include sending an automated SMS or email notification, triggering a siren or locking a door, or escalating the alarm to the priority operator queue. Step 4 — Close and Log: Every workflow must end with a Closing Status such as False Alarm or Police Dispatched. This ensures cleaner reporting and a complete audit trail.
The Operator Alarms Page The Alarms page is the central cockpit for monitoring staff. The left side displays all unassigned alarms that have arrived but have not yet been picked up by an operator. The right side displays columns for each online operator, showing their current active workload and status — Online or Offline. Bell Icon: Triggers a manual test alarm to verify workflow logic. Phone Icon: Manually starts a Call Workflow when a customer contacts the monitoring center directly.
Automated vs. Manual Workflows Use automated workflows for technical signals such as low battery alerts. If the issue does not resolve after a set time, configure the workflow to escalate to a manual workflow for human intervention. This keeps operators focused on situations that genuinely require their attention.

title: What is Evalink Talos?
excerpt:
What is Evalink Talos? Evalink Talos is a sophisticated, cloud-based alarm management platform designed specifically for monitoring stations and enterprise security teams.
Overview In the NXGEN ecosystem, Talos serves as the Alarm Verification and Di
content:
What is Evalink Talos? Evalink Talos is a sophisticated, cloud-based alarm management platform designed specifically for monitoring stations and enterprise security teams.
Overview In the NXGEN ecosystem, Talos serves as the Alarm Verification and Dispatch Engine. While GCXONE handles the video analytics, device management, and AI processing, Talos provides the workflows operators use to process alarms, contact customers, and document incidents. Together, GCXONE and Talos form a complete security solution — GCXONE detects and filters events using AI, and Talos ensures the right operator takes the right action at the right time.
Core Capabilities Intelligent Alarm Processing: Talos handles real-time alarm reception and prioritizes alarms based on custom business logic. This ensures that a Panic Button alert always reaches an operator before a Loitering alert. Intuitive Operator Interface: The Talos dashboard is optimized for high-stress environments. Operators get one-click actions for rapid incident closure, embedded GCXONE video streams directly inside the alarm card, and mobile access for managers to review site status and history from any iOS or Android device. Reporting & Compliance: Every action taken by an operator — from opening an alarm to calling a technician — is logged in an immutable audit trail, ensuring full compliance with industry standards.
Key Benefits 80% False Alarm Reduction: Integrated AI filters out environmental noise before alarms reach operators. Site Auto-Sync: Sites created in GCXONE appear in Talos automatically. Centralized Admin: Manage users and permissions across both platforms from one place. Zero Infrastructure: No on-premise servers or maintenance required. Scalable Pricing: Pay only for sites that are actively being monitored. Faster Training: The intuitive UI reduces onboarding time for new operators.
